transcript.scene: A minimalist figure wearing a blue cap watches a sunset in silhouette against a deep blue sky with yellow and orange gradients. The layout progresses from one large panel to increasingly smaller panels, visually representing introspective and fragmented thoughts.
transcript.text:
SOMETIMES, WHEN I'M ALONE WATCHING A SUNSET...

...I FEEL LIKE I'M WATCHING THE GREAT SEA OF POSSIBILITY...

...AND SOMETIMES I BELIEVE THAT I CAN SEE THE WHOLE SEQUENCE OF MY LIFE BEFORE ME...

...MEANDERING AIMLESSLY LIKE AN ABSTRUSE GOOSE...

...SEARCHING FOR FULFILLMENT IN THIS CORRUPT SUBSET OF EXISTENCE.

MY LIFE MUST HAVE A CONVERGENT SUBSEQUENCE SOMEWHERE... OUT THERE...

WHERE THERE'S A HIGHER FUNCTION...
WHY CAN'T I PROVE IT?
transcript.visualNote: The progressively shrinking panel sizes mirror the character's mental spiral into increasingly abstract and obsessive philosophical questioning, creating a visual representation of overthinking.

transcript.scene: Black and white stick figure comic set at headquarters. Two characters monitor a computer workstation as they receive and decode a mysterious signal from space.

THERE.

THIS HAS GONE TOO FAR.
♪ NEVER GONNA GIVE YOU UP ♪ NEVER GONNA LET YOU DOWN... ♪

transcript.scene: Two round-faced characters in simple black and white line art. One delivers increasingly technical criticisms of string theory while the other's patience deteriorates, culminating in physical violence.
transcript.text:
...FIRST OF ALL, STRING THEORY HAS NOT MADE A SINGLE TESTABLE PREDICTION IN OVER 30 YEARS.

BY MAKING STRING THEORY RIDICULOUSLY MALLEABLE WITH YOUR 10TH DIFFERENT WAYS TO COMPACTIFY THE EXTRA DIMENSIONS...

...YOU ESSENTIALLY PUT THE THEORY BEYOND THE REACH OF ANY CONCEIVABLE EXPERIMENTAL TEST.

SECOND OF ALL, STRING THEORY IS ONLY FORMULATED PERTURBATIVELY. A FULL NON-PERTURBATIVE DEFINITION OF THE THEORY DOESN'T EXIST.

THIRD, STRING THEORY DESCRIBES PERTURBATIVE EXPANSIONS ABOUT FIXED SPACETIME BACKGROUNDS.

ANY RESPECTABLE FUNDAMENTAL THEORY OF QUANTUM GRAVITY WOULD BE BACKGROUND INDEPENDENT.

FOURTH, IN STRING THEORY, THE DIRAC OPERATOR AND THE GAUGE FIELDS ARE...

PAK

YOU KEEP TALKING LIKE A BITCH, I'M GONNA SLAP YOU LIKE A BITCH.
transcript.visualNote: The humor stems from an abrupt tonal shift from six panels of calm technical physics criticism to sudden violence with a slapping sound effect.

transcript.scene: A character sits at a desk surrounded by mathematics textbooks, reading dense academic proofs. The simple line-drawn art style captures the character's expression shifting from focused reading to frustration.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: "I'VE ALWAYS SUSPECTED THAT THIS IS HOW MATHEMATICS TEXTS ARE WRITTEN."

Panel 2: "Define a point p in a metric space X to be a *condensation point* of a set E ⊂ X if every neighborhood of p contains uncountably many points of E."

Panel 3: "Suppose E ⊂ ℝ^k, E is uncountable, and let P be the set of all condensation points of E."

Panel 4: "THEOREM: P is perfect and P^c ∩ E is at most countable."

Panel 5: "PROOF OF THEOREM:"

Panel 6: "HONEY, LOOK WHAT I'M WEARING."

Panel 7: "FWAP"

Panel 8: "DAMN YOU, WALTER RUDIN... ...AND GODSPEED."

Bottom right: "Left as exercise."
transcript.visualNote: The character ignores their partner's seduction attempt while engrossed in the textbook, then throws the book in frustration. The omitted proof steps represent the textbook practice of leaving derivations for readers to complete.

transcript.scene: University of Pennsylvania, 1946. Stick figures discuss the historical facts of ENIAC, a pioneering computer, in a simple black-and-white style with occasional color highlights. The comic ends with a modern twist showing contemporary computer use.
transcript.text:
UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA, 1946...
ENIAC
IT CONTAINED 17,468 VACUUM TUBES, 7,200 CRYSTAL DIODES, 1,500 RELAYS, 70,000 RESISTORS, AND 10,000 CAPACITORS.

ENIAC
ENIAC WAS THE WORLD'S FIRST GENERAL-PURPOSE ELECTRONIC COMPUTER.
ENIAC WAS PROGRAMMED BY LABORIOUSLY MANIPULATING SWITCHES AND CABLES BY HAND. EVEN SIMPLE CALCULATIONS TOOK DAYS TO PROGRAM.

ENIAC
IT WEIGHED 30 TONS AND TOOK UP 680 SQUARE FEET OF SPACE.
IT CONSUMED 150W OF POWER AND EVERY TIME IT WAS SWITCHED ON, THE STREET LIGHTS OF PHILADELPHIA FLICKERED.

IT TOOK ALMOST 3 YEARS TO BUILD AND IT COST OVER $500,000.

ENIAC
THE ENGINEERS WERE LATER QUOTED AS SAYING...

...TOTALLY WORTH IT, BITCHES!
transcript.visualNote: The final panel shows stick figures playing a video game on a television, contrasting the massive, calculation-focused purpose of ENIAC with modern entertainment use of computers.

transcript.scene: A six-panel stick-figure comic spanning from heaven to Earth 15 billion years in the future. God and angels prepare to create the universe, then humans struggle with particle physics equations.

Angels: "YES, LORD."

transcript.scene: A black and white stick figure webcomic set in the LHC control room where physicists face an expanding micro black hole crisis. Actor Wil Wheaton unexpectedly arrives to help solve the problem.
transcript.text:
DESPITE ASSURANCES FROM PHYSICISTS AROUND THE WORLD, THE UNTHINKABLE HAS HAPPENED. EARLIER TODAY, THE LARGE HADRON COLLIDER IN SWITZERLAND HAS CREATED A STABLE MICRO BLACK HOLE. THE SITUATION IS GROWING. NOW IT'S THREATENED TO DESTROY THE ENTIRE LHC FACILITY... AND THEN...

EARTH!

MEANWHILE, THE WORLD'S TOP MINDS GATHERED AT THE LHC IN AN ATTEMPT TO SOLVE THE PROBLEM.

ACCORDING TO OUR DATA, THE BLACK HOLE WILL BEGIN TO EXPAND EXPONENTIALLY IN 47 SECONDS. THEN NOTHING CAN STOP IT.

I THINK I CAN HELP.

WIL WHEATON?!

IS THIS SOME KIND OF JOKE, SON?

I'M WITH SAG. WE DON'T JOKE.

NOW THE WAY I SEE IT, THE BLACK HOLE IS FEEDING OFF OF A MATTER STREAM FROM A RESIDUAL PROTON CLOUD DRIFT IN THE CHAMBER.

THE FIRST THING YOU NEED TO DO IS FILL THE CAVITY WITH BARIUM. INJECT AN ELECTRON BUNCH AT EXACTLY THE RIGHT VELOCITY TO CREATE A PLASMA LIKE FIELD. ACCELERATION GRADIENT OF 1.2 GV/M. THAT SHOULD BE SUFFICIENT TO DISRUPT THE MATTER STREAM.

NO PROBLEM. I'LL JUST CALCULATE IT IN MY HEAD RIGHT NOW. I'LL ENTER THE DATA HERE... DONE!

IT'S WORKING! THE BLACK HOLE IS BEGINNING TO EVAPORATE!

YOU DID IT, WIL WHEATON! YOU SAVED THE PLANET WITH ONLY 3 SECONDS TO SPARE!

AND HERE'S SOME COMPLIMENTARY COPIES OF MY BESTSELLING BOOK, 'THE HAPPIEST DAYS OF OUR LIVES'.

NOW YOU FINE LADIES AND GENTLEMEN CAN GET BACK TO YOUR HOLE GUEST OF UNLOCKING THE MYSTERIES OF THE UNIVERSE. HAPPY HIGGS BOSON HUNTING!

AND REMEMBER - NEVER FORGET TO CHECK YOUR REFERENCES.

THANKS, WIL WHEATON!

YOU SAVED US! YOU SAVED US ALL!

*SIGH*

THAT KID'S NOT SO BAD AFTER ALL.

MAKE NO MISTAKE ABOUT IT. WESLEY CRUSHER MAY HAVE SUCKED, BUT WIL WHEATON RULES!
transcript.visualNote: Wil Wheaton solves an impossible physics problem in his head while the world's leading physicists cannot.

The style is simple line-drawn with minimal detail, depicting the monster attacking the characters while a narrator describes the events with increasing absurdity.

SO THE THREE HAPPY FRIENDS WERE JUST SITTING THERE, MINDING THEIR OWN BUSINESS...

OMG! HE'S EATING MR e³!

transcript.scene: A grid of 936 small gray dots arranged left-to-right, top-to-bottom, with scattered colored dots marking significant life milestones. Below is explanatory text and a legend.
transcript.text:
LAST MONTH WENT BY PRETTY QUICKLY, DIDN'T IT? ONE MONTH IS NOT A VERY LONG TIME, BUT THE FACT OF THE MATTER IS THAT THE LIFE EXPECTANCY (IN THE UNITED STATES) IS ONLY ABOUT 936 MONTHS. LIFE SURE LOOKS DIFFERENT TO ME WHEN I CAN SEE IT OF MY LIFE DISPLAYED BEFORE ME AS 936 LITTLE BLOBS.

IF EACH BLOB REPRESENTS ONE MONTH OF YOUR LIFE TAKEN SEQUENTIALLY FROM LEFT-TO-RIGHT, TOP-TO-BOTTOM, HERE ARE SOME INTERESTING MILESTONES/STATS:

🔴 MOZART WRITES HIS FIRST SYMPHONY AT THIS AGE.
🔴 CONGRATULATIONS! YOU ARE NOW A WOMAN/MAN.
🟣 AVERAGE AGE OF WOMEN AT FIRST CHILD BIRTH.
🔵 EINSTEIN PUBLISHES PAPER ON SPECIAL RELATIVITY AT THIS AGE.
🟢 AVERAGE AGE OF FIRST MARRIAGE
🟠 AYN RAND PUBLISHES 'THE FOUNTAINHEAD' AT THIS AGE.
🔴 SORRY, YOU ARE NO LONGER ELIGIBLE FOR THE FIELDS MEDAL.
🟢 JOHN F. KENNEDY BECOMES 35th PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES AT THIS AGE.
🟡 AVERAGE AGE OF FIRST TIME GRANDPARENT
🔵 YOU MADE IT, MANY OF YOU ARE NOW ELIGIBLE FOR SOCIAL SECURITY BENEFITS AT THIS AGE.
⭕ YOU'RE ON BORROWED TIME NOW.

NOTE: WHERE APPROPRIATE, THE STATISTICS PERTAIN TO THE POPULATION OF THE UNITED STATES, BUT THE NUMBERS ARE VERY SIMILAR IN MANY OTHER PARTS OF THE WORLD.

NOW GET OFF YOUR ASS AND DO SOMETHING THIS MONTH!
transcript.visualNote: The finite grid of 936 dots makes human lifespan concrete and visually finite. Colored dots mark major achievements, emphasizing how few months contain significant milestones.

transcript.scene: A family meal scene with three simplified figures seated at a table. The comic uses minimal black and white line art to depict a prayer that escalates from traditional gratitude to graphic descriptions of industrial meat processing.

DEAR LORD, WE THANK YOU THAT WE CAN COME TOGETHER AS A FAMILY EVERY YEAR FOR FOOD, FELLOWSHIP, AND FUN.

transcript.scene: A man in a blue shirt sits on a stool speaking with three circle-headed figures in a 3x3 grid of minimalist line-drawn panels.
transcript.text:
This is what I heard.
GOOD SIR, I FIND IT QUITE INEXPLICABLE.

WHY DO YOU INSIST ON VIEWING TV DRAMAS INVOLVING CEREBRAL PLOT LINES INVOLVING THE IMAGINING OF PROBABLE FUTURE WORLDS?...

WORLDS BASED ON LOGICAL EXTRAPOLATIONS OF CURRENT SCIENTIFIC AND CULTURAL TRENDS? I BELIEVE THIS TO BE INDICATIVE OF LATENT HOMOSEXUAL TENDENCIES...

...WHICH, BY IMPLICATION OF MY VOCAL INTONATION, I DEEM TO BE A SOCIALLY UNACCEPTABLE LIFE-STYLE CHOICE IF ACTED UPON.

ME? I MUCH PREFER WATCHING A GROUP OF BUFF MEN IN TIGHTS ENGAGING IN A MASSIVE HOMOEROTIC DOGGIE PILE

...ALL WHILE IN THE COMPANY OF MY SLIGHTLY-INEBRIATED, MALE-ONLY FRIENDS.

IN FACT, I HAVE AN INSATIABLE URGE TO DO SO RIGHT NOW...

SO, WITHOUT FURTHER ADO, I TAKE MY LEAVE OF YOU, GOOD SIR.

HA HA HA HA

But I'm pretty sure *this* is what he said.
DUDE, WHY ARE YOU WATCHING STAR TREK? THAT'S SO GAY!

C'MON, GUYS. LET'S GO WATCH SOME FOOTBALL.

HA HA HA HA
transcript.visualNote: The speaker hypercorrects crude mockery into elaborate formal language, then reveals the actual casual insult was much simpler. The irony: the mocker's own preference for watching football with male friends is equally homoerotic as the activity he ridicules.

transcript.scene: Two stick-figure characters with circular heads discuss a mathematical discovery across nine panels in simple black and white line drawings.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:

Black character: "CHECK IT. BE PREPARED TO BE AMAZED. THIS IS GONNA BLOW YOUR MIND."

Panel 3:
Black character: "THIS IS GONNA TOTALLY BAKE YOUR NOODLE. THIS IS GONNA..."

Panel 4:
"10² + 11²"

Panels 5-9:
"10² + 11² + 12² = 13² + 14²"

Panel 9:
Blue character: "HOLY SHIT."
transcript.visualNote: The setup uses grandiose language about Calabi-Yau manifolds and promises of mind-blowing revelation, but the reveal is a simple integer equation: 10² + 11² + 12² = 13² + 14². The anticlimactic contrast between complex mathematical terminology and elementary arithmetic creates the humor.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic progressing from a solitary figure on a hillside at night through scientific and technological imagery before returning to the original scene, using black and white line drawings with occasional color accents.
transcript.text:
IS IT POSSIBLE TO READ THE MIND OF GOD?

GOD, IF YOU'RE THERE, SHOW ME A SIGN.

WOW! WHAT A COINCIDENCE

Is it possible to read the mind of God?

We have been asking that question since the dawn of human history. It is this inquisitive nature that defines us. It is this inquisitive nature that propels and compels us. It compels us to try to understand the universe.

It compelled our ancestors to contemplate the mysteries of mathematics.
ΑΙΤΙΟΜΑΤ-
ΜΗΔΕΙΣ ΕΙΣΙΤΩ

It compelled us to build soaring monuments with remarkable feats of engineering.

It compelled us to calculate the motion of heavenly bodies.

...and to unlock Nature's most stubborn secrets.

Today, that same curiosity propels the frontier of our knowledge and technology.

Every day, we get better at harnessing more energy and manipulating it to our bidding.

...while, at the same time, we're getting better at manipulating more with less energy.

How long will it be before we are compelled to merge with our technology, mind & body?

Will there ever be a time when we can no longer distinguish ourselves from our technology?

...or individuals from each other?

Then as we infuse the world around us with more of our technology, aren't we, in fact, infusing it with ourselves?

Surely this infusion of our intelligence will expand throughout our solar system, and beyond.

When we reach that point where we can manipulate all of the energy and matter in the universe,

...when we can manipulate space and time to do our bidding,...

...will we, then, finally be able to read the mind of God?

Perhaps we never had a
transcript.visualNote: The coincidence shown is visually represented as a shooting star or meteor in response to the character's request for a sign, creating an ironic commentary on pattern recognition and faith.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure character walks alone through their neighborhood on Christmas Eve at night, depicted in minimalist black lines with occasional yellow accents. The narrative traces their journey from quiet melancholy to defiant emotional release.
transcript.text:
I have an annual holiday tradition.

Every Christmas, at night...

...I take a walk around my neighborhood alone.

It's the loneliest night of the year.

The streets are never emptier than on Christmas night.

Sometimes, I peer into the windows of the houses I pass.

Everybody seems so happy on this special night, surrounded by the warmth of family and friends.

...as if the universe refuses to acknowledge my existence.

One time, I saw a young couple walking the streets, too,...

...lost in their own cocoon of happiness and joy, completely oblivious to my presence.

It makes me feel... insignificant.

ACKNOWLEDGE MY EXISTENCE! / ACKNOWLEDGE MY EXISTENCE!

I EXIST, DAD GAMMI!!!

I may be insignificant...

MERRY F*CKING CHRISTMAS!

...but the universe will NOT ignore me.
transcript.visualNote: The character's gestures and movements become increasingly erratic and frantic as they transition from quiet desperation to explosive defiance.

= 3.7×10^34 J

DARK FORCE (ENERGY)
S = ∫d⁴x√-g[¹⁄₁₆πR - ½gᵘᵛ∂ᵤφ∂ᵥφ - V(φ)]
∂ + ½∂̄∂ + ³⁄₈ = 0, V= g²/φ
P = 4π·7×10^25 W

transcript.visualNote: The humor derives from rigorous physics calculations showing that the Death Star's destructive power is negligible compared to the theoretical energy of the Force, making its planet-destroying capability statistically insignificant.

transcript.scene: A stick figure in black and white. A robber with a gun pursues a victim through an escalating confrontation, which then extends to a 911 dispatcher and finally a parent explaining a lesson to a child.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: (Silent)

Panel 2:
Robber: "STICK 'EM UP!"

Panel 3:
Victim: "PLEASE DON'T SHOOT, JUST TAKE MY WALLET!"
Robber: "RELAX. I DON'T WANT YOUR MONEY."

Panel 4:
Robber: "I WANT INFORMATION."

Panel 5:
Robber: "WHAT DOES THE FISCHER-GRIESS MONSTER LOOK LIKE?"
Victim: "WHAT?"

Panel 6: (Silent)

Panel 7:
Victim: "WHAT COUNTRY ARE YOU FROM?"
Robber: "WHAT?"

Panel 8:
Victim: "'WHAT' AIN'T NO COUNTRY I EVER HEARD OF. THEY SPEAK ENGLISH IN 'WHAT'?"
Robber: "WHAT?"

Panel 9:
Robber: "ENGLISH, MOTHERFUCKER! DO YOU SPEAK IT?!"
Victim: "YES!"

Panel 10:
Robber: "THEN YOU KNOW WHAT I'M SAYING. DESCRIBE THE FISCHER-GRIESS MONSTER!"
Victim: "WHAT?"

Panel 11:
Robber: "SAY 'WHAT' AGAIN! I DARE YOU! I DOUBLE-DARE YOU, MOTHERFUCKER! SAY 'WHAT' ONE MORE GODDAMN TIME!"

Panel 12:
Victim: "I-I-IT'S A FINITE GROUP."
Robber: "GO ON!"

Panel 13:
Victim: "IT'S THE LARGEST OF THE SPORADIC GROUPS."
Robber: "DOES IT LOOK LIKE A BITCH?"

Panel 14:
Victim: "WHAT?"
(Robber shoots: "BANG")

Panel 15:
Victim: "OOOOH"
(Robber walks away)

Panel 16:
Victim (on phone): "911 EMERGENCY. HELP, I'VE BEEN SHOT."

Panel 17:
Dispatcher: "WE'LL SEND HELP RIGHT AWAY, BUT FIRST, WHAT'S THE ORDER OF THE MONSTER GROUP?"
Victim: "WHAT? HOW SHOULD I KNOW?"

Panel 18:
Dispatcher: "I'M SORRY, SIR. THIS LINE IS RESERVED FOR PEOPLE WHO KNOW ELEMENTARY GROUP THEORY."
Victim: "WHAT?"
(*CLICK*)

Panel 19:
Dispatcher (to young child): "AND THAT IS THE REASON, SON."

Panel 20:
Parent: "THAT'S WHY YOU NEED TO LEARN MATH."

Panel 21:
Parent: "NOW GO TO YOUR ROOM AND DO YOUR HOMEWORK."
transcript.visualNote: The humor relies on the victim repeatedly saying "WHAT?" in response to questions about the Fischer-Griess Monster, a mathematical concept; the dispatcher shooting the victim for continuing this pattern; and the punchline framing this as a cautionary tale for why children must study mathematics.

transcript.scene: A six-panel black and white stick-figure webcomic showing two characters in conversation, where one repeatedly asks 'What is X?' in response to explanations. Yellow caption boxes mark time passages as the dialogue progresses.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
"...THEN YOU NEED TO INVOKE THE COMPILER AND..."
"WHAT'S A COMPILER?"

Panel 2:
"WELL,... IN THIS CASE, A COMPILER TRANSLATES SOURCE CODE INTO OBJECT CODE..."

Panel 3:
"...WHICH IS THEN PASSED TO A LINKER WHICH TURNS IT INTO MACHINE CODE."
"WAIT... WHAT'S MACHINE CODE?"

Panel 4:
"MACHINE CODE IS THE CODE THAT CAN BE DIRECTLY EXECUTED BY THE CPU OF THE..."
"WHAT'S A CPU?"

Panel 5:
"THE CPU IS A MICROPROCESSOR THAT..."
"WHAT'S A MICROPROCESSOR?"

Panel 6:
"10 minutes later..."
"...SO THE POSITIVE CHARGE ATTRACTS ELECTRONS FROM P-TYPE SILICON THAT SEPARATES THE..."
"WHAT'S AN ELECTRON?"

Panel 7:
"30 minutes later..."
"...SO ACCORDING TO THIS THEORY, SYMMETRY BREAKING OCCURS AT ABOUT 100 GeV..."
"WHAT'S A GeV?"
transcript.visualNote: Each answer to 'What is X?' prompts another question, creating a recursive chain descending from programming concepts through hardware to fundamental particle physics.

Panel 17: [Wordless]

'I AM LOOKING.'

'NO YOU'RE NOT; STOP LYING TO ME, LOOK AT ME, DAMMIT.' 'BUT...'

comics.title: Practical Zen
comics.comment: One windy day two monks were arguing about a flapping flag. The first said, "I say the flag is moving, not the wind." The second said, "I say the wind is moving, not the flag." A third monk passed by and said, "The wind is not moving. The flag is not moving. Your minds are moving." --- Zen koan
comics.commentHtml: One windy day two monks were arguing about a flapping flag. The first said, "I say the flag is moving, not the wind." The second said, "I say the wind is moving, not the flag." A third monk passed by and said, "The wind is not moving. The flag is not moving. Your minds are moving." --- Zen koan

transcript.scene: A minimalist outdoor scene with grass and sky. Three figures in traditional dress discuss the nature of motion, with one holding a flag on a pole while two others sit observing.
transcript.text:
IS THE FLAG MOVING?... OR IS THE WIND MOVING?

I SAY THE FLAG IS MOVING; NOT THE WIND.

I SAY THE WIND IS MOVING; NOT THE FLAG.

NO. IT IS NEITHER THE WIND NOR THE FLAG THAT IS MOVING.

IT IS YOUR MINDS THAT...
transcript.visualNote: The flag-holder is knocked backward by wind while explaining that neither the wind nor the flag is moving, only the observers' minds.

transcript.scene: A character with a blue circular head writes in a diary across multiple panels in minimalist black and white line art, progressing from writing at a desk to lying alone in bed.
transcript.text:
DEAR DIARY,

TODAY I RAN INTO ALICE, THE SNOBBY TEES GIRL, AT STARBUCKS.

I INTRODUCED MYSELF AND WE TALKED AND LAUGHED FOR HOURS.

SHE INVITED ME BACK TO HER PLACE FOR "A DRINK—A NIGHT OF REALLY DIRTY SEX."

SHE UNDRESSED ME—TORE OFF MY CLOTHES WITH HER TEETH...

...AND WE MADE LOVE—WENT AT IT LIKE WILD ANIMALS IN HEAT.

WE DID IT BY A ROARING FIREPLACE ON A RUG MADE OF BEAR SKIN...

...7 TIMES!

I KEPT TELLING HER THAT I'M NOT A MACHINE...

...BUT SHE WAS ALL LIKE, "C'MON, DO ME ONE MORE TIME, YOU MAGNIFICENT BASTARD!"

BY THE WAY,...

IF YOU'RE WONDERING WHY I'M WRITING TOMORROW'S ENTRY TODAY,...

...IT'S BECAUSE I BELIEVE THE FUTURE IS UNWRITTEN AND THAT WE CREATE OUR OWN DESTINY.

NOT TONIGHT. I'VE GOT A BIG DAY TOMORROW.
transcript.visualNote: The character is revealed to be writing tomorrow's diary entry in advance as a fantasy while lying alone in bed, contradicting the elaborate fictional account of the sexual encounter.

comics.title: Disclosure
comics.comment: Help me!
comics.commentHtml: Help me!

transcript.scene: A stick-figure character with a blue circular head sits at a computer in the first panels, then is confronted by another stick figure in later panels. The comic uses a minimalist black and white style in a 3x4 grid layout.
transcript.text:
LAST MONTH MY LITTLE WEBCOMIC HAD A RECORD 500,000,000* VISITS.
*I USE BASE 5

NOW THAT MY READER BASE IS LARGE ENOUGH, I THINK I SHOULD EXPLAIN WHY I REALLY DRAW COMICS.

I FEEL I MUST DISCLOSE SOME IMPORTANT INFORMATION TO THE PUBLIC, AND A WEBCOMIC SEEMED LIKE A GOOD WAY TO GAIN AN AUDIENCE.

FOR YEARS, I HAVE BEEN PART OF A SHADOWY UNDERWORLD OF BLACK OPS AND GOVERNMENT COVERUPS.

THE WORLD IS ACTUALLY CONTROLLED BY AN ELITE GROUP OF EVIL MEN WORKING IN UNDERGROUND BASES OWNED BY GOOGLE.

THESE EVIL MEN, IN TURN, ARE BEING TELEPATHICALLY CONTROLLED BY A GROUP OF EVIL REPTILIAN ALIENS FROM THE ALPHA DRACONIS SYSTEM.

THESE ALIENS CARE NOTHING FOR HUMAN LIFE; BUT ONLY WANT TO CONTROL OUR VAST SUPPLY OF COWS.

THEY ARE SYSTEMATICALLY TRYING TO REDUCE THE HUMAN POPULATION BY SPRAYING POISONOUS CHEMTRAILS, FLUORIDATING OUR WATER, AND...

THERE HE IS! GET HIM!

YOU BLABBED, QUAID! YOU BLABBED ABOUT GOOGLE!

YOUR ASS IS OFFICIALLY ERA—
transcript.visualNote: Panel 1's asterisk and footnote reveal the visitor count is misleading, using base 5 instead of base 10. Panel 11's text cuts off mid-word, leaving the sentence unfinished as the character is interrupted.

comics.title: Dear Higgs Boson
comics.comment: It seemed only fair .
comics.commentHtml: It seemed only fair.

transcript.scene: A single-panel letter on plain white background with black border. A small worried stick figure appears in the bottom right corner.
transcript.text:
Dear Higgs Boson,

We know you're out there. We can feel you now. We know that you're afraid. You're afraid of us; you're afraid of change. We don't know the future. We didn't write this to tell you how this is going to end. We wrote this to tell you how it's going to begin.

As you know, our Large Hadron Collider has had some setbacks due to a.... uh... "transformer malfunction" but we know it was you. You sabotaged our machine. We hope you've been enjoying your vacation because we're scheduled to restart in September 2009 and we pissed.

...so run and hide, asshole. Run and hide. If you should get careless and allow yourself to get detected by the Tevatron, we are going to be supremely disappointed; because we want to find you first, and when we do, rest assured we are not going to publish right away. We're going to teach you some manners first.

Love,

CERN
transcript.visualNote: The Higgs Boson is personified as a sentient being capable of sabotage, with CERN delivering threats in the style of an action film. The stick figure's worried expression reinforces the comedic menace.

transcript.scene: A four-panel mathematical proof presented in simple black and white line drawings, featuring a stick figure and progressively deteriorating underwear, concluding with a theorem and proof statement below.
transcript.text:
Assume that your new underwear is a multiply connected space.

Over time, it will become "even more" multiply connected.
THIS FACT HAS BEEN VERIFIED TO A HIGH DEGREE OF ACCURACY BY YEARS OF EXPERIMENTAL EVIDENCE BY THE AUTHOR.

Eventually, the holes begin to merge and it becomes doubly connected.
I WOULD STILL TOTALLY WEAR THIS.

In the end, the waistband will snap (trust me).

THEOREM: Simply connected underwear is simply unwearable.

PROOF: Left as exercise for the reader.
transcript.visualNote: Underwear progressively becomes more tattered with additional holes across panels. A statement "I WOULD STILL TOTALLY WEAR THIS" accompanies visibly deteriorating underwear. Final panel shows the waistband torn into a jagged, ribbon-like shape. Stick figure shrugging in bottom right corner.

comics.title: The String Theory Landscape
comics.comment: Dontcha know? April is national poetry month in the U.S. and Canada.

transcript.scene: A poem in handwritten-style font on white background, followed by an illustration of three angry stick figures with pitchforks chasing a fleeing figure wearing glasses.
transcript.text:
OR, A VISION IN A DREAM
A FRAGMENT

In Academia did theoretical physicists
A stately string theory decree:
Where Branes, the sacred rivers, ran
Through compactified dimensions measureless to man
Down to a stable vacuum.
So twice 5 x 10⁴⁹⁹ configurations of fertile ground
With walls and towers were girdled round:
And there were minima bright with sinuous rills,
Where blossomed many a solution-bearing tree;
And here were Calabi-Yau manifolds ancient as the hills,
Enfolding sunny spots of positive vacuum energy.

But oh! That deep romantic chasm which slanted
Down the [Greene] hill of refutability!
A savage place! as holy and enchanted
As e'er beneath experimental result was haunted
By the specter of falsifiability!

And from this chasm, with ceaseless turmoil seething,
As if theoretical physics in fast thick pants were breathing,
A mighty fountain momently was forced:
Amid whose swift half-intermitted burst

And thesis advisors everywhere did live in mortal fear,
Since angry grad students by the hundreds did throng:
As mathematical evidence came e'er so clear,
That string theory indeed is wrong.
transcript.visualNote: Angry graduate students with pitchforks chasing a fleeing physicist, illustrating the final lines about advisors living in mortal fear of angry grad students.

transcript.scene: A multi-panel composition contrasting ornate Victorian engraving-style illustrations with minimalist line-drawn figures, shifting between dramatic 19th-century dialogue about domestic despair and a contemporary reader's detached commentary.
transcript.text:
Well, I'll get the divorce and be Vronsky's wife.

What, then? Will Kitty stop looking at me as she did today? No. And will Seryosha stop asking or thinking about my two husbands?

And between me and Vronsky what new feeling will I think up?

Is anything - not even happiness but just torment - possible?

No, nothing! Impossible! Our lives are parting ways.

and I have become his unhappiness and he is mine,...

and it is impossible to remake either him or me.

AHHH... 'ANNA KARENINA'. A CLASSIC.

SUCH A TRAGIC STORY... HOW ANNA KILLS HERSELF.



SPOILERS: Tragic in any century.
transcript.visualNote: The humor contrasts the intense emotional despair of the novel's existential crisis with a modern reader casually spoiling the plot while reading in bed, treating literary tragedy as casual entertainment.

I'M FREE!!! I FEEL SO FREE, LIKE I'M FLOATING ON THE CLOUDS, I AM A LEAF ON THE WIND, THERE ARE NO LIMITS TO THE POSSIBILITIES OF MY LIFE...

transcript.scene: A stick-figure webcomic with multiple panels showing a conversation between characters in simple black line drawings on white background. The narrative parodies action movie tropes and undercover cop scenarios with absurdist humor.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: "ooh... here comes Chad. He's so cute. Please notice me. Please. Please."

Panel 2: "Outta my way, dorky / poor / androgynous girl." "OOF"

Panel 3: "FREEZE!!!" "What?!!"

Panel 4: "Surprise! I'm an undercover cop" "I thought you were my boyfriend." "I am your friend, but I'm still gonna bang your sister."

Panel 5: "You're a good guy who just happened to get mixed up in crime. I'm not after you. I'm after the evil kingpin who killed my family."

Panel 6: "And there he is!!"

Panel 7: "TACKLE"

Panel 8: "Police! You're under arrest!" "You idiot! I'm DEA. You just ruined 5 years of undercover work."

Panel 9: "You're off the force, Chad, and by that I mean secretly work on the case independently to redeem yourself."

Panel 10: "Oh, say hi to Amber, the ridiculously hot chick who becomes inextricably linked with your improbable adventures."

Panel 11: "C'mon, let's go kill bad guys. It's a good thing you're not wearing a bra under that thin white t-shirt."

Panel 12: "Who are you?" "I'm a stereotypical member of an underrepresented urban minority that gets caught up in a stereotypical world of Caucasians."

Panel 13: "oh... uh..." "haha. It's so awkward when white people try to emulate the vernacular of today's urban youth. It's funny." "ok, dawg."

Panel 14: "Hey, what's that scary ominous sound coming from that dark room? Let's investigate just in case it is that psychotic serial stabber." "CREAK" "BUMP" "RUSTLE" *STAB* *STAB* *STAB* "AAAAAAAHHH!"

Panel 15: "Whew! Thank God he only punctured my spleen. At least I can still kill 300 bad guys with 6 bullets and then inspire a group of underdogs to exceed their expectations."

Panel 16: "But first I have to spontaneously hack into the FBI's central database without getting detected and then warn the world about the impending doom that only I know about."

Panel 17: "And afterwards, I will mentor you in the ways of the world to guide you out of your psychological apathy. After all, I have a beard and I'm old so obviously I'm wise." "cool."

Panel 18: "Hey, you're the sad pathetic loser from the beginning of the comic..."

Panel 19: "...but, without your glasses, you have suddenly transformed into a smokin' hot hottie. I'd totally tap that."

Panel 20: "Then I totally forgive you for being a douchebag. Kiss me, you fool, and use excessive tongue so it's real uncomfortable to watch." "AHHH" *MMMPHA*

Bottom (upside down): "Awesome: all the lulz on"
transcript.visualNote: The character's transformation from sad pathetic loser to smokin' hot hottie is indicated solely by the removal of drawn glasses, with no other visual change to the figure.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic set in a front yard with grass. A person interacts with a red wagon filled with flowers across multiple panels, using crude stick figures and basic object outlines.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: "Sometimes I plant flowers in my little red wagon and leave it in my front yard."

Panel 2: "Then I wait for a bee to discover it."

Panel 3: "Flush with the taste of sweet nectar, the bee rushes back to its hive." [Sound effect: "BZZZZZZ"]

Panel 4: "By performing an intricate dance, the bee can communicate to its friends the location of the flowers."

Panel 5: "But before he returns, I take the flowers away."

Panel 6: "...and wait behind some bushes."

Panel 7: Person: "When the bee returns with all his friends, he's all like WTF." Bee: "WTF?"

Panel 8: "And his friends are all like WTF." [Multiple bees]: "WTF?"

Panel 9: Person: "I just like embarrassing him in front of his friends." Person: "IT WAS RIGHT HERE. WHATEVER, DUDE."

Panel 10: "I'm a dick."
transcript.visualNote: The humor depends on the bees performing their waggle dance to communicate the flower location to hive-mates, only to find the wagon empty when they return with their friends.

transcript.scene: A nine-panel instructional comic in black and white with brown and red accents using simple line drawings to depict the hierarchical breakdown of fabric structure from macro to micro scale, concluding with a small figure and an apple.
transcript.text:
Millions of fibers interwoven.

Each fiber composed of millions of chains of molecules.

A downward force works to separate the fibers.

...and the fibers begin to separate...

...slowly...

...slowly...

Until, finally, a single molecule of a single chain of a single fiber loses its battle ...and breaks free. POP

...causing a chain reaction. RIP

[Red background, fabric breaking]

[Apple hanging from a string]

[Small figure being hit by apple] BONK

A single molecule can change the course of history.
transcript.visualNote: The final panels depict an apple falling and striking a small figure, referencing Newton's discovery of gravity and humorously equating a single molecule breaking in fabric with a major scientific revelation.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure comic with a blue-headed narrator recounting a story in the style of an adult magazine's reader-submitted letters section, shifting unexpectedly from sexual encounter to theoretical physics discussion.
transcript.text:
Dear Penthouse Forum, I never thought this would happen to me.

I was at the Van Pelt Library just minding my own business.

Suddenly, this mysterious woman with wild unkempt hair approached me.

I had no idea who she was but we ended up in bed together. I CALL THIS THE VENUS BUTTERFLY.

She was like a ferocious animal ... completely uninhibited.

In fact, I'm pretty sure that some of the things we did are still illegal in 49 states. SAY MY NAME, BITCH!

Afterwards, we just started speaking casually about grand unification using string-network condensates.

Soon we had a unified string-net picture of fermions and gauge bosons.
⊕(▢—▢—) = ⊕(▢▢▢)
⊕(▢—▢—) = ⊕(▢—▢)

We worked furiously through the night working out the masses of all the fermions.

This indirectly allowed us to reconcile the black hole information paradox with our theory of string-network condensates!

Completely exhausted, we both collapsed like wave functions into a state of deep slumber. Z Z Z Z     Z Z

When I awoke, she was gone - like a phantom that disappeared into the luminiferous aether. I never even learned her name.

All I had left to remember the previous night's erotic outburst was the paradigm-altering theory that we formulated together.

Please see the attachment to view the final theoretical results.

One way or another, I always get my results published.
transcript.visualNote: Sudden tonal shift from sexual narrative to theoretical physics discussion; quantum mechanics terminology and equations integrated into the narrative structure; sleeping character depicted with Z symbols.

transcript.scene: A 3x3 grid of simple stick-figure panels showing a bespectacled programmer's progression through thousands of days, from learning programming fundamentals to acquiring advanced knowledge in physics and biology.
transcript.text:
Days 1 - 10
"Teach yourself variables, constants, arrays, strings, expressions, statements, functions,...."

Days 11 - 21
"Teach yourself program flow, pointers, references, classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, ...."

Days 22 - 697
"Do a lot of recreational programming. Have fun hacking but remember to learn from your mistakes."

Days 698 - 3648
"Interact with other programmers. Work on programming projects together. Learn from them."

Days 3649 - 7781
"Teach yourself advanced theoretical physics and formulate a consistent theory of quantum gravity."

Days 7782 - 14611
"Teach yourself biochemistry, molecular biology, genetics...."

Day 14611
"Use knowledge of biology to make an age-reversing potion."

Day 14611
"Use knowledge of physics to build flux capacitor and go back in time to day 21."

Day 21
"Replace younger self."

As far as I know, this is the easiest way to "Teach Yourself C++ in 21 Days".
transcript.visualNote: The protagonist uses decades of advanced knowledge in physics and biology to build a time machine, travel back to Day 21, and replace their younger self, creating a paradox that suggests completing C++ in 21 days requires time travel preparation.

transcript.scene: A diary entry comic in simple black and white line art style featuring a young student narrator documenting incidents from a typical school day. The art is minimalist with stick-figure-like characters.
transcript.text:
Dear Diary,

Today was just another typical day at school. Let me just say for the record that middle school is the dumbest idea ever invented. You get gorillas like me who need to shave twice a day mixed in with little pipsqueaks whose testicles haven't dropped yet.

OUTTA MY WAY, N00BS!

Before homeroom, I snuck into the janitor's closet with Kelly Forkes and Jen Fallon where we played a good game called Seven Minutes in Heaven.

Sure, I'm only in middle school, but I still get alotta nookie for my age. I guess it comes with the territory – me being captain of the wrestling team and all. God, I love being a jock.

SHIP-SEKI!

During third period, we had a math test so I made sure to sit behind this Korean kid. I heard that those people are good at math and stuff.

LOL FAG.

During lunch period, I ran into this loser kid named Greg or something. I can't stand him cuz he's always hanging out with this dorky fat kid like they wuz in love or something.

Anyway, I used the lunch money to buy a six-pack and I got drunk in the woods behind the school with a bunch of high school kids.

All in all, it was a pretty good day at school.

I'd like to see this book written and the pink one banned from libraries.

comics.title: The Smartest Man in Babylon
comics.comment: In the beginning was the command line...
comics.commentHtml: In the beginning was the command line...

transcript.scene: A minimalist educational webcomic with stick figures and simple line drawings on white backgrounds. The narrative progresses from abstract mathematical concepts through cosmic philosophy, featuring binary code sequences, a Turing machine diagram, a photograph of Earth in space, and a computer file path.
transcript.text:
Today I want to talk to you about a long, but finite, sequence of random ones and zeroes. This sequence is thousands and thousands of digits long. Part of this particular sequence looks like this:

[Binary code block]

It certainly seems random, and so it is. One can almost imagine that this sequence was produced by repeatedly flipping a fair coin. Now you can't touch or feel this sequence of numbers like it was a real object, but it is definitely real.

It lives as if life in the Platonic ether, floating freely and happily along with an infinite number of other sequences and other mathematical constructs - like space-filling curves, Klein bottles, and infinite dimensional manifolds. It has an absolute existence independent of any human mind able to conceive or contemplate it. They all come from 'The Book'.

[Turing machine diagram]

In fact, if you look far enough into the binary representation of pi, you would most probably find this exact sequence embedded somewhere in there.

π = 11.001001000011111...

This random sequence of numbers existed before you were born. It existed before the storming of the Bastille. It existed before Caesar crossed the Rubicon. It existed before the Egyptians built the great pyramids. And before the dinosaurs roamed the Earth.

And because its existence is abstract - transcending space and time - it most likely existed at the beginning of the universe.

Now an amazing thing about this sequence is that you can turn it into almost anything by feeding it into a machine. You could build a machine that would turn it into the third movement of Beethoven's 10th. You could build a machine that would turn it into a moving series of pictures. Or you could build a machine that would turn it into a general-purpose operating system - and someone has.

[Image of Earth against black space background]

Now the single 'smartest' (and most brazen) thing that any person has ever done... ...was to convince the world that this abstract sequence of numbers was his property.

C:\GENIUS!
transcript.visualNote: The final panel presents 'C:\GENIUS!' as a computer file path, evoking the earlier explanation that binary sequences can be decoded into operating systems, creating a metaphor for claiming ownership of fundamental reality.

comics.title: A Mini Tutorial
comics.comment: I'm gonna have to side with Insane Clown Posse on this one. Magnets ARE a miracle. Magic everywhere in this bitch.
comics.commentHtml: I'm gonna have to side with Insane Clown Posse on this one.  Magnets ARE a miracle.  Magic everywhere in this bitch.

transcript.scene: An educational infographic with white background, black text, and line drawings explaining magnetism. The layout flows top-to-bottom with explanatory text blocks interspersed with diagrams of magnets, wire coils, atoms, and electrons.
transcript.text:
The phenomenon of magnetism was first observed at least 2500 years ago in small fragments of magnetized iron ore. The earliest known surviving descriptions of magnets show that such phenomena were known to the people of ancient Greece, India, China, and parts of Asia Minor. These strange stones attracted to and repelled only objects like iron and nickel. It was observed that rubbing a magnet with another magnet would sometimes cause the previously non-magnetized object to become magnetized. It was also observed that when magnets were placed near certain other objects that previously exhibited no magnetic properties, the target object would also become magnetized.

Today we know that a magnet produces an invisible magnetic field in the space around it and a second magnet responds to that field. This magnetic field can be visualized as invisible magnetic field lines radiating from the north pole.

However, it should be kept in mind that the direction of the field lines is just a convention and does not imply that the magnetic field is actually moving.

Magnets always have a north and south pole. In other words, you can't break a magnet in half to obtain a magnet with only one pole; left as is you might wish just a south pole. The two smaller pieces would, in fact, become separate magnets, each with its own north and south poles.

Similarly, if you join the north pole of one magnet with the south pole of another magnet, the two magnets would act as a single magnet with a single north pole and a single south pole.

A magnet's magnetic field can be described by a vector quantity called the magnetic dipole moment. The strength of the magnetic field at any given point is proportional to the magnitude of the magnetic dipole moment and decreases with the distance from the magnet. You can visualize the magnetic field as a torque which tends to align the magnetic moment along the lines of the field. In the case of a bar magnet, you visualize the magnetic moment by imagining the vector pointing from its south pole to its north pole.

Magnetic fields are created by moving electric charge (such as moving electrons or protons). For example, when a current moves through a wire, it produces a magnetic field around the wire as illustrated below.

If you wind the wire into a coil as illustrated below, the magnetic field will reinforce itself.

As you can see, a coil with a current running through it becomes sort of like a bar magnet (called a solenoid) with a north and south pole. The more times you wind the coil, the stronger the magnetic field produced. You can make the magnet even stronger by winding the coil around a core of magnetic material (Yay, congratulations! You just built a kind of magnet called an electromagnet).

Every electron has its own magnetic moment due to an intrinsic property of the electron called spin. Spin is a quantum mechanical property of the electron but you can think of it as being similar to a classically spinning sphere.

Electrons not only spin; they also revolve around the nucleus of an atom. It was once thought that electrons orbited around the nucleus the same way that planets orbit around the sun but today we know that the picture is far more complex (involving concepts from quantum mechanics that won't concern us here). Nevertheless, this orbital motion of the electron also gives rise to a magnetic moment.

The atoms of certain elements have acquired electrons in their outer orbitals which can cause the atom to become an unpaired orbital. This unpaired electron's magnetic moment tends to line up all aligned. Such materials are called ferromagnetic. Under normal conditions (i.e. no externally applied magnetic field) the magnetization of the domains are aligned randomly and hence, the material has no net magnetic field. However, when an external magnetic field is applied, the domains tend to align themselves parallel to the field, thus magnetizing the material.

To make a long story short,

transcript.text:
Cold, hungry, wet... I don't know how much longer we can take this. Eating worm-ridden biscuits and rotten meat. Dysentery, small pox, fever... Half of us are bleeding from our feet from walking miles in the snow with no shoes. Many of us have grown despondent and think we should just give up. Man alive, the sleet is really pounding down now. Should we be victorious, I wonder how history will remember us. Will future generations remember all the hardships we've endured and the sacrifices we've made? How will our great great great great great great grand children remember this moment?

[Gallery panel - no dialogue]
transcript.visualNote: The contrast between the speaker's detailed account of suffering, disease, and despair in the top panel and their romanticized, heroic depiction in the painted artwork viewed in the gallery below.

transcript.scene: A nine-panel stick-figure comic in a 3x3 grid using minimalist black line art. The narrative follows a college student named Mitch through a series of escalating misadventures and poor decisions.
transcript.text:
I had this friend in college named 'Mitch'.
I'M MITCH

Mitch really liked to party. Sometimes too much.

One time, he drank too much and threw up a little in his beer cup.

And then he just continued to party - drinking out of that same cup.

During our junior year, he got wasted, stole his girlfriend's car, and crashed it into a tree. (Thank God no one was hurt.)

When we bailed him out of jail, he had cuts and bruises all over his face... but not from the crash.

When he was getting arrested, he tried to run from the police while still wearing handcuffs. He tripped and fell face first into some thorn bushes.

I haven't seen Mitch since graduation. The last I heard, he was going to medical school.

MORAL OF THE STORY: NURSE, SCALPEL! The less you know about your doctor, the better.
transcript.visualNote: The irony of Mitch's reckless and accident-prone behavior throughout college contrasted with his becoming a physician.

transcript.scene: A multi-panel autobiographical webcomic in simple stick-figure style with blue and black ink. The narrator recounts a relationship story from college, spanning from an awkward phone call through meeting a girl in person, a first kiss interrupted by a roommate, and reflections on the encounter.
transcript.text:
Years ago in college I was having an awkward conversation with my parents about a girl I liked. I was too shy to ask her out, so I happened on the next train to Princeton.

At this time, I had not yet met her parents and I didn't know anything about her family, but I did know that she was a girl and that was enough. When she answered the phone, I asked for her to meet me on the train.
HI
WELL, I CAN'T ASKED STUPID QUESTIONS
WHAT ARE YOU DOING?
I'M ON THE TRAIN

I had had that I didn't think it was a good idea for me to meet her. She said she lived just down the street from me. So I rode a call to come pick me up at the train station.
WANT TO MEET YOU ON THE TRAIN
UM... WAIT, DO YOU?
IF I TELL YOU THAT I'M ON THE TRAIN... IS THAT BAD?

Finally, I convinced her girlfriend to leave the house and we took a walk on the beach near her place. It was nice. We walked back through the brick subway-like brakes. She was awkwardly holding all of the pieces of beach and tried to pick one up.
LOOK AT ALL THESE INTERESTING SORTS OF LOVES
THAT'S... THAT'S KIND OF WEIRD
WELL

For the next three hours she showed me all of the interesting parts of town, pointing it round little alleys, and we shared the exact the beach and places she used to go. When we got back to her place, she asked me if I wanted to come in. We ended up on the couch and just started chatting. Then, suddenly, she became very nervous and I realized that she wanted me to kiss her. So I did it. Her roommate walked in. It was awkward.
THIS IS WHERE I HUNG OUT MOST IN HIGH SCHOOL
SO I JUST THOUGHT, UM... WELL... MAYBE YOU COULD KISS ME?
WAIT, ARE YOU—
OH MY GOD, I'M SO SORRY, I DIDN'T KNOW YOU TWO WERE—
NO, NO, IT'S FINE

For some reason, she seemed really excited to show me her life. She offered to drive me to the train station. But that trip is where things really went wrong. We were kissing between kiss idea, who then tried to open my car door while we were making out. She grabbed my arm really hard.
WAIT, KIND OF MUSIC DO YOU LIKE?
I REALLY LIKE THIS BAND
OH, THEY'RE PRETTY GOOD, COOL
YEAH, YOU SHOULD LISTEN TO THEM

On the train ride home, so I pondered the day's events. Maybe I'm just a timeless robot with the heart. She, remember that episode of Star Trek: The Next Generation where Data didn't know if he was in love? When we got...
THING WAS UP ON MY HEAD
CELL PHONE, HUH HOW ARE THOSE DAYS? MY PHONE IS POWERED BY INSECTILE QUANTUM COMPUTERS AND SO YOUR PHONE... CONTINUES TO DRIVE MY CORE PROCESSORS, THEREBY EXTENDING MY MEMORY

...but something like that. I got all the episodes mixed up in my head. But I didn't know what happened. I had a really, really weird feeling about our relationship. Like there was something fundamentally broken about it. I remembered that my previous girlfriend moved a little too fast. Why too early in our relationship. Maybe this was different?

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick figure comic in black and white depicting various social interactions between characters in scenarios involving science, physics, and everyday situations.
transcript.text:
So the beta decay from the cesium-137 is monitored by a detector interfaced to the computer. The software then converts the time intervals between pairs of decay events into random bits. If a certain sequence of 1s and 0s is produced, it activates this laser which, in turn, sweeps across the Casimir cavitation. When the arc reaches the top, it switches on this Van de Graaff generator which levitates this mylar origami unicorn!

Wait... what?
Unicorn?

What the hell did you build this device for again?
What?
Does this thing have any useful applications?
It... levitates unicorns.

Wanna help me finish my thanksgiving leftovers?
Nah. I'm trying to watch my weights.

Do you believe that the Standard Model of particle physics is correct?
Um... sure. I guess so. Why?
Then mass is probably just a mirage... the illusory consequence of our swimming in an ocean of Higgs field permeating the universe.

greater rationalization skills

Hey, have you read this article? Researchers at CERN successfully trapped antihydrogen.
Holy cow! Those bastards finally did it. This is amazing. This is—

OMG OMG OMG OMG.
What's the matter with you?

Nothing.
I'll be in my bunk.
eww

frequent nerdgasms

Honey, do we have any D batteries for the flashlight?
Strictly speaking, they're not 'batteries.' They're cells.

more opportunities to be an elitist dick
transcript.visualNote: The comic presents scenarios where scientific knowledge or pedantry enables rationalization (e.g., Higgs field explanation for mass allows weight-watching dismissal) and nerd enthusiasm (e.g., antihydrogen discovery prompts character to retreat to bunk). Visual progression shows consequences of science education on social behavior.

transcript.scene: A black and white sketchy comic depicting a post-apocalyptic world with ruined cities, smoke, a solitary figure amid destruction, and a partially buried Statue of Liberty in a barren landscape.
transcript.text:
For years I tried to warn them but they wouldn't listen.

And then the terrible times came.

At first, the people of Earth simply squabbled over the dwindling resources.

Then came war, and famine,... and pestilence.

Now anarchy reigns and all of the social progress and technological achievements of humanity have collapsed into a ruinous heap.

Savage bandits now roam the slum-ridden streets of formerly magnificent cities, scavenging and murdering for the few remaining scraps in order to survive.

I am writing this in the hopes that civilization will once again rise from the ashes; in the hopes that humanity will start again and rebuild itself.

To any future civilization that may read this, please do not repeat our mistakes. Please heed my warning.

Please use 128-bit IP addresses from the beginning.
transcript.visualNote: The apocalyptic warning concludes by attributing humanity's downfall to failure to adopt 128-bit IP addresses (IPv6), treating internet address exhaustion as a civilization-ending catastrophe.

comics.title: barcode
comics.comment: I'm like that guy that always suspects that his wife is cheating on him - simply because he once did it himself.
comics.commentHtml: I'm like that guy that always suspects that his wife is cheating on him - simply because he once did it himself.

transcript.scene: A single-panel webcomic in black and white line art showing a stick figure attempting to destroy a barcode from an Amazon package box through increasingly elaborate and paranoid methods.
transcript.text:
Carefully peel off barcode from package box.

Shred.

No wait. That's stupid. A barcode scanner only needs a thin slice of the whole block to interpret it.

Anyone with a smartphone would still be able to read my UPS tracking number.

Maybe I should shred it vertically.

But that black line at the top would make it easy for anyone to determine the orientation of the strips.

I should cut out the top line first.

And then shred it vertically.

But if the top is cut out at an angle, then it would be easy to line up the strips in the correct order by height.

Then any Joe Shmoe with a scanner could read my tracking number, social engineer their way to my Amazon account, and find out that I read a lot of click lit. Unacceptable.

It's best to cut off the ends of the strips at random angles and heights.

Or maybe I should just burn it. It's the only way to be sure.

I have a debilitatingly irrational fear of dumpster divers.
transcript.visualNote: The barcode undergoes increasingly fragmented destruction methods across panels, with the final panel showing the stick figure holding a lighter, conveying manic desperation through escalating visual chaos.

transcript.scene: A step-by-step mathematical solution to a linear differential equation displayed on a white background with black border. Cat images serve as placeholder variables throughout the system of equations.
transcript.text:
Solve:
y''' + 2y" - 5y' - 6y = 0,    y(0) = y'(0) = 0,    y"(0) = 1

m³ + 2m² - 5m - 6 = 0

m = -1, m = 2, m = -3

y = [cat₁]e⁻ˣ + [cat₂]e²ˣ + [cat₃]e⁻³ˣ

[cat₁] + [cat₂] + [cat₃] = 0

-[cat₁] + 2[cat₂] - 3[cat₃] = 0

[cat₁] + 4[cat₂] + 9[cat₃] = 1

[cat₁] = -1/6

[cat₂] = 1/15

[cat₃] = 1/10

y = -1/6 e⁻ˣ + 1/15 e²ˣ + 1/10 e⁻³ˣ
transcript.visualNote: Different cat images replace the constants [cat₁], [cat₂], and [cat₃] throughout the equation system, making the solution a literal puzzle of determining what values the cats represent.

transcript.scene: A 3x3 grid comic in simple black and white stick figures presenting a satirical argument progressing from evolutionary biology to modern lifestyle desires, ending in contradiction between stated primal ambitions and actual behavior.
transcript.text:
Millions of years of primate evolution.

Animal instincts are embedded deep in our DNA.

We are explorers; we are hunters; we are natural born killers.

We were not designed to sit in quiescent passivity in a cubicle.

We were not designed to live in concrete jungles infused with our insidious technologies.

I want to live free; to live off of the wilderness.

I don't want to suppress my animal instincts; I want to release my inner killer.

I want to hunt my own food.

I want to taste the fresh kill in my mouth.
transcript.visualNote: In the final panel, the figure is biting a book rather than actual prey, creating a visual pun that undercuts the grandiose declarations about releasing primal hunting instincts with the mundane reality of consuming books.

transcript.scene: Nine-panel minimalist comic with stick figures (circles with dot eyes) on white backgrounds. Characters progress from neutral expressions to distressed faces by the final panels.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: "You may be alone now, but there is hope."

Panel 2: "There is a theory that says that, for each one of you, there is a partner for you somewhere out there."

Panel 3: "Your partner simply hasn't been found yet."

Panel 4: "It doesn't matter what you look like; it doesn't matter whether you're attractive or not."

Panel 5: "It doesn't matter how much you weigh; whether you're big or small."

Panel 6: "It doesn't matter what your personality is like; whether you're charming or strange."

Panel 7: "According to this theory, there is a partner out there for each and every one of you."

Panel 8: "Unfortunately, there is no compelling evidence to support this theory yet."

Panel 9: "So SUSY is probably wrong and you're all SOL."
transcript.visualNote: The punchline contrasts the encouraging, inclusive tone of panels 1-7 with the blunt pessimistic conclusion in panels 8-9, reinforced by characters' expressions shifting to distressed in the final panel.

transcript.scene: A person in black and white line art explains daily clock discrepancies throughout their house, progressing from observations to theoretical calculations to an absurd conclusion involving a UFO.
transcript.text:
Every morning, I synchronize my bedroom clock with the coordinated universal time scale maintained at NIST.

My clock is supposed to be accurate to within 1 second a day but, sometimes, I notice that it differs from UTC(NIST) by as much as 10 seconds by the end of the day.

I also noticed that the same thing happens with the clock in the living room, the clock in the kitchen, and every other clock in the house.

Based on several days' worth of observations of these daily time discrepancies in my house, I was able to use my 'vast' knowledge of general relativity to extrapolate a possible contour map of the required gravity anomalies necessary to cause the time discrepancies.

Based on the contour map, I can make an educated guess as to the general shape of the gravity warp drive.

WAIT, WHAT?

The gravity warp drive on the UFO that's been visiting me. Obviously.

OH, RIGHT.

A little knowledge is a dangerous thing.
transcript.visualNote: The character casually invokes general relativity and gravity field theory to explain minor clock errors, then reveals the punchline that a UFO with a warp drive is the obvious culprit.

comics.title: Nocturne
comics.comment: Sometimes people mistake my awesomeness for cockiness.
comics.commentHtml: Sometimes people mistake my awesomeness for cockiness.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure character explains a profound realization about artistic connection across time while listening to Chopin, then shares this insight with a friend who responds with increasing dismissal.
transcript.text:
Chopin composes the nocturne in Paris in 1832.

Ashkenazy plays his own interpretation of it in London in 1981.

And now i'm listening to the recording of it here in Philadelphia in 2011.

So the very thoughts of these men have combined into one and is now pouring into my brain through my ears. It's like the three of us – all great men – are somehow interconnected across space and time.

It's like Chopin, Ashkenazy, and I are bros.
[Diagram showing: Chopin — me — Ashkenazy]

DOESN'T THAT JUST BLOW YOUR MIND ?

(silence)

WHATEVER, DUDE.

(silence)

JEALOUS ?
transcript.visualNote: The friend's dismissive responses (indifference, "whatever," and suggesting jealousy) undercut the speaker's elaborate philosophical moment about connecting with great artists across time.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic in six panels spanning 1903 to 2011, drawn in simple black lines with brown/tan ground and black space, depicting a century of aviation and space travel history culminating in a gravestone.

The first controlled, powered, and sustained heavier-than-air human flight covered a distance of just 37 m.

Panel 2 (1927):
The first non-stop flight from New York to Paris, just a little over 20 years later, covered 5800 km.

Panel 3 (1969):

* SIGH *

(Gravestone with "2011" and space shuttle etched on it)
transcript.visualNote: The exponential technological progress of panels 1–3 (37 m → 5,800 km → 350,000 km) contrasts sharply with panel 5's resignation and panel 6's gravestone, implying stagnation in space exploration rather than continued advancement.

transcript.scene: Two stick-figure characters sit at tables facing each other in a minimalist black-and-white setting. One character with a large head conducts a social experiment on the other character wearing glasses, applying scientific methodology to an unexpected social interaction.
transcript.text:
THE HUMAN BRAIN IS A SEETHING CAULDRON OF ELECTROCHEMICAL REACTIONS.

IN ORDER TO DETERMINE THE INNER WORKINGS OF THIS BLACK BOX, IT MUST BE SUBJECTED TO DISTAL STIMULI.

THEN THE EXPERIMENTER MUST MAKE CAREFUL OBSERVATIONS OF THE SUBJECT'S RESPONSES TO STIMULI.

THIS IS THE VERY CRUX OF THE SCIENTIFIC METHOD, THE BODY OF TECHNIQUES FOR ADVANCING SCIENTIFIC KNOWLEDGE.

A MOST NOBLE PURSUIT.

ADMINISTER STIMULI NOW.

HEY THERE.

GET LOST, CREEP.

TIME TO COLLATE MY DATA.
transcript.visualNote: The experimenter treats a hostile social rejection as valid experimental data, maintaining scientific detachment despite the awkward interaction.

transcript.scene: A nine-panel stick figure comic showing three characters—an armored figure, a tall stick figure, and a tall figure with black hair—investigating hobbit tracks. "soon..."

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic showing a conversation between a child and their ancestors spanning millions of years of evolutionary history, progressing from recent family stories to prehistoric times.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
Child: "What's the point of going on?"
Adult: *SIGH*

Panel 2:
Child: "I'm surprised at you"
Another character: "What?"

Panel 3:
Adult: "Who are you guys?"
Adult 2: "We are your ancestors"

Panel 4:
Adult 1: "Do you have any idea what you just put through to get you here?"
Character: "Huh?"

Panel 5:
Adult: "Take, for example, your grandmother here."

Panel 6:
Caption: "She was an aspiring young scientist at a time when few women were able to enter the field. She was forced to leave her career hopes behind due to power."

Panel 7:
Caption: "She ended up working for poverty wages in a textile factory in Chicago."

Panel 8:
Caption: "It was in the Windy City that she met and fell in love with Enzo, the baker."

Panel 9:
Adult: "A year later, your father was born. And you were born 31 years later."
Child: "Uh...I don't see—"
Adult: "And *this* is your great great grandfather."

Panel 10:
Caption: "He fought in the Civil War."

Panel 11:
Caption: "He lost his leg in a campaign to take the Shenandoah Valley from the Confederate forces."

Panel 12:
Caption: "2 years later he married your great great grandmother. And you were born 117 years later."

Panel 13:
Caption: "When the Normans invaded England, your great⁹ grandmother was with child living in a small village near Hastings. Her husband was killed in battle and she fled into the forests where she subsequently gave birth to your great⁸ grandmother."

Panel 14:
Child: "And you were born 915 years later."
Child: "I still don't see the point of—"

Panel 15:
Caption: "Your great¹²⁸¹¹⁴ grandfather was killed while defending his mate and child from a saber-toothed cat."
Sound effects: "RUN, LUCY, RUN!"

Panel 16:
Caption: "...and you were born 3,269,127 years later."

Panel 17:
Caption: "Your great⁵⁸⁸⁸¹⁴ grandfather lived in a scary world dominated by large cold-blooded killers. He supported his family by foraging for bugs while hiding from fearsome predators."

Panel 18:
Caption: "...and you were born 216,933,475 years later."

Panel 19:
Caption: "In fact, all of our ancestors participated in this grand adventure called life, each with a unique story to tell and a distinct challenge to overcome. Yet all of them—facing the dangers and barely surviving long enough to produce offspring. In this way, they've managed to successfully continue this magnificent chain of life."

Panel 20:
Adult: "...all of this just to give you the opportunity to live."

Panel 21:
Adult: "You are the end result of billions of years of struggle, sacrifice, despair, death, and amorous relations. You are the latest result of an unbroken chain of evolutionary victories from the very first single-celled organisms all the way down to you."

Panel 22:
Child: "So what are you going to do with this gift of yours for which each of us have struggled so hard? Hmm?"

Panels 23-25:
[Silent contemplation, final panel fades to black]
transcript.visualNote: Timeline visualization progresses from recent family photographs and historical periods through increasingly distant prehistoric eras, depicting early mammals and ancient creatures to emphasize the vast span of evolutionary history.

WELCOME, MY CHILD.
WOW, God?!

I have so many questions!
Of course you do.

I'd be happy to answer any...
Show me a proof of the Riemann Hypothesis!

Riemann, huh? No problem. After all, I literally wrote 'The Book'. LOL

Now let's see here. Riemann... Riemann... Riemann...

Ah, here we go. 'The Riemann Hypothesis'.

LEFT AS EXERCISE

OK, no problem. Let's see...
(Mathematical equations and notation scribbled on pages)

(Complex mathematical notation)
If we... um... OK... we need to... um... Fourier inversion... and...ur... converges...

What if I...ur... hmm, that won't work... hmph, what a stumper...

haha. It's like you created an object so heavy that even you can't lift it. hahaha

(Character kicked out of clouds with WHAM sound effect)

DAMMIT.
transcript.visualNote: God answers with 'LEFT AS EXERCISE,' a textbook phrase deflecting proofs to the reader, used literally as the answer to an unsolved mathematical problem. The character points out the paradox that God, supposedly omniscient, cannot solve what God created.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic with simple geometric backgrounds in shades of green, blue, and black. Six panels arranged in a grid show a narrative progression using stark black silhouettes, concluding with a large turkey silhouette at the bottom.
transcript.text:
Our land was a bountiful land filled with awe-inspiring beauty.

We roamed free over rolling green hills and pristine fields of grass.

And then THEY came. The giants. A most grotesque alien race from a far away place.

They occupied our land. They came like locusts with their hideous technology and terrible weapons. They arrived in ships so large it boggled the imagination. And they started to slaughter my people without mercy.

My people were herded into wretched prison camps where they could do nothing but wait to die.

But the most dreadful time is 'The Festival', the time of year when the aliens perform their ritual slaughter of our people by the millions. And before they consume our flesh, they perform a most sadistic ceremonial chant to their god.

Yet some of us still roam free beyond the power of the aliens. We few, we band of freedom fighters, will carry on the resistance. We will not vanish without a fight. We will not rest until *all* of our brothers and sisters can once again fly free.

AMERICAN HISTORY
by Turkey
transcript.visualNote: The comic presents American colonial history from a turkey's perspective, with the turkey narrator describing European colonizers as alien invaders, Thanksgiving as a genocidal festival, and turkeys as freedom fighters.

So I used to go clubbing a lot with my friend Chucky.
[CHUCKY]
He has got to be the worst dancer I've ever seen... ever!

But he always runs to the center of the dance floor and just dances all by himself.

WTF?

Either he doesn't know how stupid he looks, or he just doesn't care.
Either way, it saddens me to know that I will never be that free.

transcript.text:
One night, I was having trouble falling asleep.

So I got up and decided to play a couple of hands of solitaire poker.

Almost immediately, I dealt myself a royal flush.
WOO - HOO !

ODDS AGAINST 649,739:1
TODAY MUST BE MY LUCKY DAY !

But then it occurred to me.

What if I just wasted my entire lifetime's supply of good luck on one useless poker hand? ...alone in my apartment.

SON OF A...



My life has sucked ever since.
NOOOOOOOOO
True story.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure webcomic depicting a man's journey from conspiracy theorist to corporate executive to disillusioned idealist. The minimalist black-and-white art style shows him progressing through various settings including office buildings and corporate environments.
transcript.text:
As I grew older, it became increasingly clear to me that the world was run by evil cabal of elites.
THEY'RE COLLAPSING OUR ECONOMY AND DESTROYING OUR FUTURE. NOBODY COULD BE THAT INCOMPETENT. IT MUST BE AN EVIL PLAN.

They lie to us and manipulate us through the mainstream media; they continually dumb us down with TV shows devoid of intellectual content.
ONLY AN EVIL CONSPIRACY COULD PRODUCE SUCH VAPID CRAPPY TV.

I concluded that we were all just slaves of the Illuminati.
ALEX JONES WAS RIGHT.

I decided that I needed to fight back.
BUT, I SHALL... BE...

FREE!

But the Illuminati can only be defeated from the inside. I needed to infiltrate their organization.

I got my foot in the door as a lowly mail clerk in one of their evil illuminational corporations.

I worked my way up the ranks with promotion after promotion...

I worked hard and made the right connections.

I studied their evil ways and learned their techniques.

Until... FINALLY... I made it to the top.

I WAS the Illuminati.

I decided not to waste any time.
SMITHERS!
YES, SIR?

I put my plan into effect immediately.
IT'S TIME TO END THIS.

IT'S TIME TO END THE CONSPIRACY OF DESTROYING THE ECONOMY AND DUMBING DOWN THE POPULATION.
THE LINE MUST BE DRAWN HERE!

THIS FAR, NO FURTHER!

BUT, SIR, THERE IS NO CONSPIRACY.

OH... OKAY.

THAT WILL BE ALL, SMITHERS.
YES, SIR.

I decided to weep for humanity.
transcript.visualNote: Silent panels create an awkward pause emphasizing the anticlimactic collapse of the protagonist's ideological mission upon learning the conspiracy does not exist.

THAT DOESN'T EVEN MAKE SENSE. NEITHER DOES RUSSELL'S PARADOX, YET IT EXISTS.
transcript.visualNote: The traveling character points out a logical paradox: if their origin universe doesn't exist in this universe's physics, they shouldn't exist either—a self-referential contradiction they resolve by referencing Russell's Paradox.

comics.title: Arithmetic for Beginners
comics.comment: I have also intentionally included fatal mistakes in the equations to make it more realistic. Please see the errata page on our website.
comics.commentHtml: I have also intentionally included fatal mistakes in the equations to make it more realistic.  Please see the errata page on our website.

transcript.scene: A webcomic panel contrasting a child-friendly arithmetic lesson with line-drawn rabbits against an abruptly complex mathematical derivation. Simple line art transitions to dense equations with advanced notation.
transcript.text:
Suppose you have one rabbit.

Now suppose someone gives you one more rabbit.

Now, if you count your rabbits, you have two rabbits. So one rabbit plus one rabbit equals two rabbits. So one plus one equals two.

1 + 1 = 2

And that is how arithmetic is done.

Now that you understand the basic idea behind arithmetic, let's take a look at a simple easy-to-understand example that puts into practice what we just learned.

**Try It Out Example 1.7**

log Π(N) = (N + 1/2) log N − N + A − ∫[N to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x,     A = 1 + ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x

log Π(s) = (s + 1/2) log s − s + A − ∫[0 to ∞] B₁(t)dt/(t + s)

log Π(s) = lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + Σ[n=1 to N] log n − Σ[n=1 to N] log(s + n)]

= lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + ∫[1 to N] log x dx − 1/2 log N + ∫[N to 1] B₁(x)dx/x − ∫[1 to N] log(s + x)dx − 1/2[log(s + 1) + log(s + N)] − ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)]

= lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + N log N − N + 1 + 1/2 log N + ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/x − (s + N)log(s + N) + (s + N) + (s + 1)log(s + 1) − (s + 1) − 1/2 log(s + 1) − 1/2 log(s + N) − ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)]

= (s + 1/2) log(s + 1) + ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x − ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/(s + x) + lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + (N + 1/2) log N − (s + N + 1/2) log(s + N)]

= (s + 1/2) log(s + 1) + (A − 1) − ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/(s + x) + lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1)/(1 + s/N) + (N + 1/2) log(1 + s/N)]

If the authors of computer programming books wrote arithmetic textbooks...
transcript.visualNote: The promised 'simple easy-to-understand example' is immediately replaced by several lines of advanced mathematical derivation involving logarithms, integrals, Bernoulli numbers, and limit notation.

transcript.scene: A minimalist illustrated adaptation of Walt Whitman's "Sea-Drift" featuring a solitary figure in green positioned throughout maritime and celestial settings. The panels alternate between colored seascapes and black backgrounds with white text, emphasizing isolation against vast horizons.
transcript.text:
In cabin'd ships at sea,

The boundless blue on every side expanding,

With whistling winds and music of the waves, the large imperious waves,

Or some lone bark buoy'd on the dense marine,
Where joyous full of faith, spreading white sails,
She cleaves the ether mid the sparkle and the foam of day, or under many a star at night,

By sailors young and old haply will I, a reminiscence of the land, be read,

In full rapport at last.

Here are our thoughts, voyagers' thoughts,
Here not the land, from land, alone appears, may then by them be said,
The sky o'eraches here, we feel the undulating deck beneath our feet,
We feel the long pulsation, ebb and flow of endless motion,
The tones of unseen mystery, the vague and vast suggestions of the briny world, the liquid-flowing syllables,
The perfume, the faint creaking of the cordage, the melancholy rhythm,
The boundless vista and the horizon far and dim are all here,
And this is ocean's poem.

Then falter not O book, fulfil your destiny,
You not a reminiscence of the land alone,
You too as a lone bark cleaving the ether, purpos'd I know not whether, yet ever full of faith,

Consort to every ship that sails, sail you!
Bear forth to them fold'd my love, (dear mariners, for you I fold it here in every leaf)
Speed on my book! spread your white sails my little bark athwart the imperious waves,

Chant on, sail on,
bear o'er the boundless blue from me to every sea,

This song for mariners and all their ships.
—Walt Whitman
1871
transcript.visualNote: The book is depicted as a ship sailing through space and ocean, literalizing the metaphor of the poem itself as a vessel. A figure in green appears at vastly reduced scale against expansive seascapes and celestial bodies, emphasizing human smallness within nature's immensity. A ship's wheel appears in one panel, mirroring the cyclical nature of voyages.

comics.commentHtml: In actuality, the entire adventure lasted about 15 minutes.  Or it never ended, depending on your point of view.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic in black ink following a 4-year-old's childhood adventure into the wilderness and reflective journey home, from exploration through various obstacles to nighttime contemplation in bed.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: "One day, when I was 4 years old, I decided that I wanted to go on an adventure in the wilderness."

Panel 2: "The first order of business was to procure a supply of food."

Panel 3: "One can never go wrong with cheese curls."

Panel 4: "With a sandwich bag full of life-sustaining cheese curls in hand, I set out into the unfamiliar."

Panel 5: "Immediately, I chanced upon what appeared to be an abandoned trail, possibly built by an ancient race long forgotten."

Panel 6: "Just imagine the adventures that occurred along this trail... lost in the sands of time."

Panel 7: "It wasn't long before the elements mercilessly tried to force my return home."

Panel 8: "Perhaps the gods have been angered by my brazen attempt to explore forbidden lands."

Panel 9: "I soldiered on. Many perils were encountered and overcome. Eventually, I reached the end of the known world."

Panel 10: "SCARY FOREST" / "END OF THE KNOWN WORLD" / "MY HOUSE" / "START"

Panel 11: "Beyond this place be mysteries begging to be explored."

Panel 12: "But first I needed rest."

Panel 13: "I found a dry patch of ground under an old board."

Panels 14-16: "And I feasted on the good food." [CHOMP]

Panel 17: "There were still many uncharted lands awaiting discovery..."

Panel 18: "...but the sun was sinking fast."

Panel 19: "And the wilderness can be an unforgiving place at night."

Panel 20: "Reluctantly, I started my long trek back home."

Panel 22: "Safe at home again. Cold and tired."

Panel 23: "Sleep did not come easy that night."

Panel 24: "For I had briefly tasted the sweet nectar of independence."

Panel 25: "And it lingered."
transcript.visualNote: The map in Panel 10 reveals the character only explored their immediate neighborhood, contrasting with the epic language describing the journey. Silhouette transition panels during the return home emphasize growing darkness and danger.
