transcript.scene: A classroom with stick-figure style characters during a literature discussion. A female student experiences an escalating internal spiral of dark thoughts about a classmate's comment, eventually ending alone in bed at night.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
"CAN ANYONE TELL ME WHAT THIS POEM MEANS?"

Panel 2:
(Silent)

Panel 3:
"YES, SEAN?"

Panel 4:
"I BELIEVE THAT THE FLOWERS IN THIS POEM REPRESENT THE BLOSSOMING OF INNOCENCE IN THE GROWING FACE OF THE DICHOTOMOUS RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN MAN AND NATURE."

Panel 5:
"THAT IS THE STUPIDEST THING I'VE EVER HEARD."

Panel 6:
"IT'S LIKE HE SIMPLY BLURTED OUT THE FIRST SEMI-SEMANTICALLY-CORRECT SENTENCE THAT POPPED INTO HIS HEAD, COMPLETELY IGNORING THE FACT THAT IT CONVEYED ABSOLUTELY NOTHING MEANINGFUL."

Panel 7:
"HOW MANY COCKTAIL PARTIES HAVE I HAD TO SUFFER THROUGH BY GETTING CAUGHT HAVING TO TALK TO BLOWHARD LIKE HIM?"

Panel 8:
"IN FACT, HOW MANY INTERNATIONAL INCIDENTS THROUGHOUT HISTORY HAVE BEEN PRECIPITATED BY CARELESS CHATTERBOXES LIKE HIM?"

Panel 9:
"MANY POWERFUL 'INSTITUTIONALIZED ORGANIZATIONS' OFTEN EMPLOY NON-THINKING MOUTHPIECES LIKE HIM TO PROPAGATE THEIR MESSAGES OF HATE AND INTOLERANCE."

Panel 10:
"FOR CENTURIES, GOVERNMENTS AND TYRANTS HAVE MANIPULATED USEFUL IDIOTS LIKE HIM TO HELP JUSTIFY TERRORISM, WAR, AND GENOCIDE."

Panel 11:
"I MUST PUT A STOP TO THIS NOW BEFORE THIS MONSTER IS RELEASED INTO THE WILD."

Panel 12:
"BUT HE'S THE ONLY STUDENT WILLING TO CONTRIBUTE ANYTHING TO THIS DISCUSSION. IF I QUASH HIS ENTHUSIASM NOW, THIS IS GONNA BE ONE HELL OF A BORING CLASS FOR THE REST OF THE SEMESTER."

Panel 13:
"HORNS OF A DILEMMA."

Panel 14:
"THAT'S A VERY INTERESTING INSIGHT, SEAN. COULD YOU PLEASE ELABORATE?"

Panel 15:
(Student looking concerned)

Panel 16:
"I AM ONE SMALL COG IN THE VAST MACHINERY OF EVERYTHING WRONG WITH THIS WORLD."
transcript.visualNote: The student's internal monologue remains unspoken while she outwardly responds politely to the classmate, creating contrast between her escalating dark thoughts and her encouraging teacher persona. Final panels show her alone in darkness in bed.

transcript.scene: A four-panel comic in black and white minimalist line art depicting two parallel scenarios: a speaker at a podium addressing a crowd in each panel. The left side shows a gaming industry presentation, the right side shows an economic policy briefing.
transcript.text:
Top left: "... SO, AS WE DEAL WITH THESE LONG-TERM OPPORTUNITIES TO PROVIDE A MORE ENGAGING GAMING EXPERIENCE, WE ARE ACUTELY AWARE OF THE FRUSTRATION EXPERIENCED BY OUR CUSTOMERS AND ..."
Crowd: "HEY! GABEN JUST TOUCHED HIS BEARD 3 TIMES!"

Top right: "... SO, AS WE ASSESS THE BENEFITS AND COSTS OF ALTERNATIVE POLICY APPROACHES, WE MUST NOT LOSE SIGHT OF THE DAUNTING ECONOMIC CHALLENGES THAT CONFRONT OUR NATION AND ..."
Crowd: "HEY! BERNANKE JUST TOUCHED HIS BEARD 3 TIMES!"

Middle left: "IT'S A SIGN!" "YEAH" "THREE! THREE! THREE!"

Middle right: "IT'S A SIGN!" "YEAH" "THREE! THREE! THREE!"

Bottom left: "NEWS - HL3 CONFIRMED"

Bottom right: "NEWS - QE3 CONFIRMED"

Caption: "The parallels are uncanny."
transcript.visualNote: Both crowds interpret an identical accidental gesture—three beard touches by the speaker—as confirmation of something significant to their respective domains: Half-Life 3 for gaming and QE3 (quantitative easing round 3) for economic policy.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic showing a race between a person and a rabbit across multiple panels with simple black ink line drawings. The narrative progresses through various terrains and obstacles, culminating in three alternate endings determined by different chance events.
transcript.text:
BANG

START

ZAP

FINISH

lucky > good
transcript.visualNote: The rabbit encounters increasingly surreal obstacles including being struck by lightning and caught in rain while progressing forward, while a slower competitor steadily advances unimpeded. Three final panels show different outcomes based on random events rather than competitors' abilities, with the faster rabbit suffering misfortune.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic with two human characters and a boxy robot with an antenna. The setting is abstract across 12 panels arranged in a 2x6 grid.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: "Hey, is that a robot?"

Panel 2: "Good boy." / "CAT"

Panel 3: "Not just any robot. THIS is the current state-of-the-art in artificial intelligence."

Panel 4: "We trained a 9-layered locally connected sparse autoencoder with pooling and local contrast normalization on a dataset of 10 million images. It was trained for 3 days on a cluster of 1000 machines comprising 16,000 cores."

Panel 5: "It can now recognize 22,000 different object categories with 15.8% accuracy."

Panel 6: "15.8%?" / "Impressive, nuh?" / "CAT"

Panel 7: "Impressive? Didn't you AI guys program a machine that defeated a world chess champion way back in 1997?"

Panel 8: "And didn't you AI guys build a computer system that kicked the respective asses of the two best Jeopardy! champions of all time?"

Panel 9: "What happened to 'HAL 9000 by 2001'? Where are our Series 800 Terminator robots?"

Panel 10: "You call this progress?!!"

Panel 11: (Silent panel)

Panel 12: "Sorry we annoyed you with our awesomeness." / "CAT"
transcript.visualNote: The robot correctly identifies a cat as 'CAT' despite achieving only 15.8% accuracy across 22,000 object categories.

comics.title: RSA-2048
comics.comment: If it wasn't for the universe and the laws of physics and stuff, I'd be unstoppable.
comics.commentHtml: If it wasn't for the universe and the laws of physics and stuff, I'd be unstoppable.

transcript.scene: A nine-panel black and white stick-figure webcomic featuring two characters—one with dark hair at a laptop and one with glasses—exploring the gap between cryptographic theory and practical computation.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
"Hey, check it out. I think I figured out how to crack 2048-bit RSA encryption."

Panel 2:
"I can do it using the general number field sieve."

Panel 3:
"On that crappy laptop? It would literally take you quadrillions of years!"

Panel 4:
"Ahh, but y'see, I'm pretty sure that I'll perfect my time machine within 20 years."

Panel 5:
"Then I can just add a function that sends the result back in time after the calculations finish."

Panel 6:
"From our point of view, it'll be like we receive the answer as soon as the calculations start. Observe."

Panel 7:
[Dark-haired character waits at laptop]

Panel 8:
"Hmmm. Nothing."

Panel 9:
[Empty panel with waiting character]

Panel 10:
"I was sure that that was going to work."

Panel 11:
"I'll bet the universe ended before the calculations finished."

Panel 12:
"Y'know, if it wasn't for real life, you'd be like a genius or something."
"Damn universe!"
transcript.visualNote: Repetitive panels showing the character waiting at the laptop with no results, visually emphasizing the passage of time and the failure of the theoretical solution through visual monotony.

transcript.scene: A stick figure sits at a desk while equations progressively cover the wall behind them across three panels. The minimalist line-drawn style shows the character's increasing frustration as formulas accumulate.
transcript.text:
E = mc²
E = m²c =
E = m·c
E = m·lose = Emkd
E = c/m = Emcml
E = √mc = E = mc²
E = m%c

The best way to have a good idea is to have a lot of ideas.
--- Linus Pauling
transcript.visualNote: The character generates increasingly absurd variations of E=mc², degrading into nonsensical formulas like 'E = m·lose.' The visual progression demonstrates Pauling's quote—most generated ideas are wrong, illustrating the cost of quantity in pursuing quality.

comics.title: Zarathustra
comics.comment: And Zarathustra spake thus: MY GOD, WE'RE LIVING IN THE FUTURE!
comics.commentHtml: And Zarathustra spake thus: MY GOD, WE'RE LIVING IN THE FUTURE!

transcript.scene: A bearded philosopher descends a mountain to teach a young woman his philosophy in minimalist stick-figure style. He is repeatedly interrupted and distracted by her use of smart glasses and social media.
transcript.text:
When Zarathustra was thirty years old, he left his home and the lake of his home, and went into the mountains.

There he enjoyed his spirit and solitude, and for ten years did not weary of it.

But at last his heart changed, and rising one morning with the rosy dawn, he went before the sun, and spake thus unto it:

Lol! I am weary of my wisdom, like the bee that hath gathered too much honey; I need hands outstretched to take it.

Zarathustra went down the mountain alone and there suddenly stood before him a young woman.

And to the young woman Zarathustra spake thus: I TEACH YOU THE SUPERMAN.
Woman: What?

Man: Man is something that is to be surpassed. What have ye done to surpass man?
Woman: ok, glass, record a video of this crazy man.

Man: The Superman is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Superman SHALL BE the meaning of the earth!
Woman: ok, glass, upload video to YouTube.

Man: Are you talking to your glasses?
Woman: Yeah. It just took a video of you and uploaded it to the internet. See?
Man: Wh-what kind of phone is that?

Man: What is all this crazy technology? How did you...
Woman: shhh, I'm video chatting with my friends.


Man: How long was I on that freakin' mountain?

Thus spake Zarathustra.
transcript.visualNote: Zarathustra's philosophical teachings about the Superman are immediately recontextualized as social media content by the woman's smart glasses and technology.

comics.title: Einstein
comics.comment: But don't worry. There are thousands of other Einsteins alive on Earth right now about whom you'll also know nothing.
comics.commentHtml: But don't worry.  There are thousands of other Einsteins alive on Earth right now about whom you'll also know nothing.

transcript.scene: A minimalist six-panel black and white comic with two blue panels featuring a silhouetted figure in different settings. The narrative contrasts an optimistic premise with the systemic barriers that prevent potential from being realized.
transcript.text:
GOOD NEWS!
The next 'Einstein' is alive and living on planet Earth right now.

She lives in a country nobody ever talks about.
...in a village nobody cares about.

She has never read a textbook.
...and has never seen the inside of a school.

Every morning she makes the long trek to the market to sell firewood.

...and every night she makes the long trek back to feed her children.

You've never heard of her.
...and you never will.
transcript.visualNote: The silhouetted figure's repetitive daily routine and isolation visually emphasize the disconnect between potential and circumstance.

comics.title: Impure Mathematics

transcript.scene: A black and white stick figure comic in a 3x3 grid where a mathematician with black hair discusses pure mathematics with a round-faced companion, escalating from academic debate to time-travel intervention and culminating in a unicorn ride. Minimalist line drawings with occasional text emphasis and visual effects.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
"So I arbitrarily defined an arbitrary mathematical structure and arbitrarily gave it some arbitrary features."

Panel 2:
"Then I arbitrarily formulated some arbitrary theorems about it."

Panel 3:
"What practical applications could this possibly have?"

Panel 4:
"There are no practical applications. That's the beauty of pure mathematics."

Panel 5:
"YOU !!!"
K₍₋ε₎ω05₍₎

Panel 6:
"I'm from the future. Come with me if you want to live!"
"What?"

Panel 7:
"In the future, your mathematical theories make possible an inter-dimensional rift in the space-time-consciousness continuum which allows Xur and the Ko-Dan armada to conquer the galaxy."
"I don't understand"

Panel 8:
"No time to explain. GET ON THE DAMN UNICORN!"

Panel 9:
[No dialogue]

Bottom caption:
"All math is applied math... eventually."
transcript.visualNote: Panel 5 contains garbled mathematical notation emphasizing chaotic interruption. Panel 8-9 show the character riding a rainbow-colored unicorn. The escalation from abstract mathematics discussion to time-travel warning about intergalactic conquest to unexplained unicorn creates absurdist visual progression.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure biblical parable comic with simple line drawings. A master figure with a blue cap distributes computers to three servants and later accounts for what they've accomplished, rendered in archaic biblical language.
transcript.text:
For the kingdom of heaven is as a man travelling into a far country, who called his three servants, and delivered unto them his goods.

And unto his three servants he gave one computer each.
And straightway took his journey.

After a long time the lord of those servants cometh, and reckoneth with them.

And the lord of the servants said unto the first servant:
What have you done with the computer in my absence?

Lord, thou deliveredst unto me a computer: behold, I have created a simple and easy-to-use graphical operating system that now runs 90% of the world's personal computers.

And his lord said unto him: Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things.

Enter thou into the joy of thy lord.
WOO-HOO

And the lord of the servants said unto the second servant:
What have you done with the computer in my absence?

Lord, thou deliveredst unto me a computer: behold, I have created a social networking site that now has over a billion users worldwide.

And his lord said unto him: Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things.

Enter thou into the joy of thy lord.
yee-ha

And the lord of the servants said unto the third servant:
And what have you done with the computer in my absence?

[The third servant sits at a desk with a laptop, unresponsive.]
transcript.visualNote: The third servant's unresponsive posture at a laptop is a visual gag implying inaction or distraction rather than productive accomplishment.

comics.title: Penultimate Author
comics.comment: ...and reagent mixers.
comics.commentHtml: ...and reagent mixers.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure webcomic set in a laboratory with medieval knight metaphors. The narrator uses contrasting imagery of knights in armor and background support staff to illustrate invisible labor in academic research.
transcript.text:
You may not always see us but we are always there.

We are the beleaguered squires toiling in the backdrop while the glorious knights in burnished armor take to the calamitous battlefield and pierce the dark forces of ignorance with bitter spears of reason.

Brandishing elegant tools of empirical illumination, they drive back the wavering darkness of superstition.

...bringing to bear the full might of logic and deductive reasoning... And we are there.

Many battles have been fought and many battles have been won as they engird their foes in an 'ever-receding pocket of scientific ignorance'* - and we are there.

*NJT

And when tales of their victories are put to paper and added to the esteemed archives of academia, our names you will see amongst those of the scholarly knights of lore.

Some Great Scientific Discovery

Authors: Polly Postdoc, Gary Graduatedstud, Obligatory Asian Dude, Larry Labtech, [ME], Pricilla Principlesinvestigator

Abstract: We made some great scientific discovery.

Introduction: Blah blah blah [repeated placeholder text]

You may not always see us but we are always there. We are indispensable journeymen in the grand adventure of scientific discovery. We are the squires of the academic knights.

We are the test tube washers
transcript.visualNote: The narrator's name in the author list requires an arrow to locate, visually reinforcing the theme of being present yet overlooked.

comics.title: The Story of the Cheese Maze
comics.comment: And the rats loved those books even more.
comics.commentHtml: And the rats loved those books even more.

transcript.scene: A black-and-white maze comic featuring four characters: two rats named Avelli and Machi, and two small humanoid creatures named Hill and Napoleon. The art uses minimal line drawings in rectangular panels with occasional larger illustrated scenes.
transcript.text:
Once, long ago in a land far away, there lived four little characters who ran through a maze looking for cheese to make them happy.

Two were rats named 'Avelli' and 'Machi' and two were littlepeople - beings who were as small as rats but who looked and acted a lot like people today. Their names were 'Hill' and 'Napoleon'.

Every day the four of them spent like little mice searching for their own special cheese.

Then one day, they each found their own kind of cheese at the end of one of the corridors.

And every morning after that, the rats and the littlepeople headed directly to the same corridor to find the good good cheese.

Until one day, the rats approached the littlepeople, pounded their tiny little bitch asses into the ground, and stole their cheese.

The rats used their newfound cheese wealth to build a machine that could create cheese out of thin air.

The machine churned out so much good good cheese, so much cheese: glorious mountains of Swiss cheese, soaring towers of Cheddar cheese, gushing rivers of delicious cheese Whiz.

And the rats built a huge wall around their cheese to keep out the littlepeople.

Sometimes the rats threw pieces of cheese over the wall. And they laughed and laughed while voraciously sucking their cheese Callahes as the littlepeople fought and clawed for every tiny morsel.

So the littlepeople learned to read - books that explained how anyone can get all the cheese they wanted if they worked hard and had a positive attitude.

The littlepeople loved those books.

THE END
transcript.visualNote: Dark comedy through juxtaposition of cute character designs with violent content. Cheese mountains and towers emphasize grotesque excess. The wall separating rats from littlepeople visually conveys hopelessness, making the final message about positive attitudes absurdly inadequate.

comics.title: alone in my room
comics.comment: And attack ships on fire off the shoulder of Orion.

transcript.scene: A minimalist black and white comic featuring a young person with short dark hair alone in their room, surrounded by mathematical equations and abstract imagery. The panels alternate between simple line drawings and detailed sketches, depicting both mundane indoor settings and surreal cosmic landscapes.
transcript.text:
Alone in my room. scribbling equations.

I've... seen things. ...things you wouldn't believe.

...terrible, beautiful things quivering on the horizon of my mind's eye.

I have seen the cold and austere universe of randomness dance in rhythm with the music of the primes at the edge of infinity.

I have run barefoot along the sinewy embankments of countless Calabi-Tau manifolds.

I have soared naked through the magnificent solitude of infinite dimensional Hilbert space and through exotic realms that have no business existing in the mundane abstractions of mortal men.

With surgical precision, I have weft, stitched, and knotted together the obscene contortions that would make angels weep.

I have collapsed stars into monstrous vortices from which nothing can escape and I have blithely skated along its pristine event horizon.

I have witnessed the birth and death of this and many other universes many times. So it goes.

Alone in my room.

With nothing but the formidable lens of mathematics and a fecundity of imagination....

I've seen some shit.
transcript.visualNote: The comic contrasts elaborate descriptions of cosmic experiences with the revelation that all these phenomena exist only through mathematics and imagination in a solitary room, culminating in crude understatement.

comics.title: pseudonymous man of mystery
comics.comment: I'm pretty sure that the real Satoshi Nakamoto would be a supreme disappointment compared to the image I've built up in my mind.
comics.commentHtml: I'm pretty sure that the real Satoshi Nakamoto would be a supreme disappointment compared to the image I've built up in my mind.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure webcomic on white background depicting a first-person character in various indoor scenes, concluding with a nighttime cityscape. The narrative uses simple black line drawings and an irreverent tone.
transcript.text:
Yes, it's true that I once forged a sword of awesomeness out of chunks of meteorites.

And yes, I once killed a ferocious bear with my bare hands to save a basket of kittens.

And every year on April 20, I make sweet, sweet love to a beautiful mermaid on a beach while wearing a dead bear's skin.

I didn't have to use my bare hands to kill that bear because I, of course, shoot deadly lasers from my ass if I want to.

But I'm a private person. I live alone in my lair of solitude atop my own personal Mount Olympus.

And in my lair, I bide my time by wisely monitoring the progress of humanity.

On occasion, when I have found that humanity was stuck in a stalemate or suffering from a stale status quo bias, I have descended from my hiding place.

...and imparted my Promethean gift of disruptive innovation to the masses.

And then, as quickly as I came, I would disappear into the dark of night like a ghost in an Audi 5000.

Out of sight from the attention of civilisation once more, I am again free to monitor the progress of humanity.

And if the people of earth should ever require my services again...

I, Satoshi Nakamoto, shall return.
transcript.visualNote: The final panel reveals a Bitcoin symbol in a spotlight beam above a nighttime cityscape.

F = G(m₁m₂)/r²

I CALL IT THE LAW OF UNIVERSAL GRAVITATION.

Y'KNOW, IT WOULD BE REALLY COOL IF, LIKE, SOMEONE COULD FIND A PRECISE VALUE FOR...

THEN I MEAN NOW! MOVE!

EXPERIMENTALISTS.
transcript.visualNote: The presenter shifts from explaining the gravitational equation to demanding immediate action from the audience as if they were servants, then dismissively refers to experimentalists—contrasting the speaker's theoretical enthusiasm with impatience for practical validation.

transcript.scene: A professor teaches quantum mechanics to a small class but is derailed by a student's increasingly elaborate philosophical questions, ultimately being reassigned to teach Philosophy 101 to a large lecture hall. Simple black and white stick figure style.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
"So these are the basic postulates of quantum mechanics that we'll be working with this semester."
[Board: "ℏ∂ψ/∂t=-ℏ²/2m∇²ψ + V(x)ψ"]

Panel 2:
"Are there any questions so far?"

Panel 3:
"Yes, Sean?"

Panel 4:
"So are you saying that quantum phenomena don't exist without the presence of a conscious observer?"

Panel 5:
"Uhh... no, that's not exactly what..."
"And you seem to be implying that the mathematical abstractions used to describe the processes are more real than the objects themselves."

Panel 6:
"I think you misunderstood what I..."
"And isn't mathematics just a man-made construct constrained by the physiological limitations of human cognitive abilities?"

Panel 7:
"Well, mathematics is just a tool that..."
"What if an alien from the Andromeda galaxy with a vastly different brain structure, and hence system of mathematics, were to develop a quantum theory based on their own mathematics? Couldn't their conception of reality possibly be completely different from our own? Who's to say that their version of reality isn't more real than ours? In fact, what is real? Maybe we should come up with a new definition of reality."

Panel 8:
[Silent panel]

Panel 9:
"I can see that there's nothing more that I can teach you."

Panel 10:
"I believe that you're ready for our advanced class."

Panel 12:
[Classroom sign: "PHILOSOPHY 101"]
transcript.visualNote: Sean's questions become progressively longer and more elaborate across three consecutive panels. The professor's advancement of the student results in reassignment from a small quantum mechanics class to a crowded Philosophy 101 lecture hall.

transcript.scene: A minimalist black and white comic set at a zoo featuring a monkey and a blue sphere character. Eight panels in a 4x2 grid show an escalating confrontation between the two as their conversation becomes increasingly heated and philosophical.
transcript.text:
Panel 2:
Monkey: "Hey, buddy, why don't you take a picture? It'll last longer."
Blue sphere: "What?"

Panel 3:
Blue sphere: "You think you're pretty hot stuff, don't you? How noble in reason, how infinite in faculty!"
Blue sphere: "?"

Panel 4:
Monkey: "Don't you look down on me, you *Homo sapiens* sumbabitch. Genetically, we're practically identical."

Panel 5:
Monkey: "What if certain random genetic mutations and millions of years of cumulative natural selection turned out just a little bit differently?"

Panel 6:
Monkey: "It could have been you with the measly 400 cc cranial capacity sitting here in this cramped prison masturbating in public and throwing poo. But nooooooooo..."

Panel 7:
Monkey: "Look at you, with your 1400 cc brain living in your big, fancy house in the suburbs listening to Mozart and reading Shakespeare getting all fat off of the fruits of your magnificent technological innovations."

Blue sphere: "DON'T YOU?!!"
Blue sphere: "I just wanted to see some monkeys."

Caption: "zoos make me sad"
transcript.visualNote: The monkey launches into an elaborate tirade about evolutionary contingency and human superiority in response to the blue sphere taking photographs, creating absurd disproportion between the innocent action and the intense philosophical response.

transcript.scene: A black-and-white stick figure comic set in a bedroom at night. One character lies in bed trying to sleep while the other becomes increasingly agitated about historical claims to the English throne, eventually shouting.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
"Hey, are you sleeping?"
"Trying to."

Panel 2:
"I have a question."

Panel 3:
"Did the Yorks really have a better claim to the throne than the Lancasters?"
"Don't care."

Panel 4:
"Sure you do. Look, I mentally constructed a family tree to figure it out."
"Don't care."

Panel 5:
"Now, if we ignore the usual laws of primogeniture and stuff and we naively restrict our analysis to autosomal DNA inheritance (which really doesn't make any practical sense except as a mathematical exercise but who cares?), the Duke of York shared about 15.625% of his genes with Edward III while Henry VI only shared about 6.25%. But of course that huge difference isn't surprising since, after all, York was a generation closer to Edward *and* he descended from two of Edward's sons.

Dooooonnnntttt caaaarrrreeeeee.

But it gets really interesting if we trace the lineage back even further. Henry VI's great grandmother, Blanche of Lancaster, was the great great granddaughter of Henry III so, according to my calculations, Henry VI shared about 1.56% of his genes with Henry III. York shared about 1.95% with Henry III. But York was killed at the Battle of Wakefield before he could take the throne. Remember? And then his son, Edward IV, became king while Henry VI was still alive. Now, Edward IV's mother was the great granddaughter of Edward III so Edward IV shared about 1.76% of his genes with Henry III. But the difference between Edward IV and Henry VI means nothing when you consider the increasing variability of autosomal DNA transmission with each successive generation. So between the years 1460 and 1471, it's a freakin' stalemate!

Pretty interesting, huh?"

Panel 6:
"I need you to get up and check my math on a calculator and confirm my facts on Wikipedia."

Panel 7:
"C'mon, get up. It's killing me. Get up!"

Panel 8:
(No dialogue)

Panel 9:
"Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up!"

Panel 10:
"GO THE F*CK TO SLEEP, BRAIN!!!"

Panel 11:
"How can anyone sleep with so many important unresolved questions in the world?"
transcript.visualNote: Panel 10 depicts the sleeping character suddenly awake and shouting in frustration. Panel 11 shows the other character calmly lying back down.

comics.title: August 4
comics.comment: Dear Diary. Today I climbed a pyramid, pretended to be Tony Montana, and I broke my foot. It was a good day.
comics.commentHtml: Dear Diary.  Today I climbed a pyramid, pretended to be Tony Montana, and I broke my foot.  It was a good day.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure webcomic in black and white with blue highlights. Two friends impulsively decide to climb a pyramid on their university campus late at night, reach the summit triumphantly, then suffer a disastrous frictionless descent.
transcript.text:
One night, Chucky and I were just hanging out in my apartment playing Soulsaifer.

CHUCKY: WHOA! WHAT?
ME: I WANNA CLIMB A MOUNTAIN.

There was nothing particularly significant about the said date, but it was August 4 of that year.

ME: WHAT?
CHUCKY: I WANNA CLIMB A MOUNTAIN LIKE RIGHT NOW.

And just like that, I dragged Chucky outside to join me on my adventure.

CHUCKY: WHERE THE HELL ARE YOU TAKING ME?
ME: ALMOST THERE.

Somewhere in the middle of the university campus stands a pyramid. I had always wanted to climb it but it was way too huge to do it by myself.

THERE SHE IS. THE SUMMIT BECKONS.

Chucky, always a good sport, immediately started racing up the side of the pyramid.

I quickly followed after.

We stood on the peak triumphant and proud.

And we both let out a primal scream in brazen defiance of God, the universe, and the winds of Fate.

ME: WHO YOU THINK YOU F**KING WITH?!? I TONY MONTANA!!!

CHUCKY: Because that's what you do when you conquer a mountain. IT'S THE LAW!

ME: YOU F**K WITH ME, YOU F**KING WITH BEST!!!

But getting to the top was easy. Getting down? Not so much. On the way down, the pyramid somehow magically transformed into a freakin' frictionless surface.

As I tried to make my way down, I slipped and started accelerating at a frightening rate.

My fall was finally broken by the tremendous impact of my feet against the hard concrete step at the bottom.

The pain was excruciating. To be honest, I probably suffered a minor bone fracture in my left foot that night.

I turned around to witness Chucky succumbing to the same fate.

In a sinister display of schadenfreude, I couldn't help but laugh as I watched Chucky falling around like a helpless turtle on its back sliding towards certain doom.

My reaction oscillated between genuine concern for my friend and pure amusement at the funny scene.

CHUCKY: HA HA... YOU OKAY...? HA...? ...HA HA...

ME: LAUNCH

As Chucky and I limped away, we passed by a couple sitting on a bench nearby.

BOY THAT WAS REALLY SMART.

ME: LIMP LIMP
CHUCKY: LIMP LIMP

Now I'll bet you're thinking that climbing a pyramid, screaming 'I Tony Montana', and breaking my foot was a pretty stupid way to spend the night.

But I'll also bet that you don't remember what
transcript.visualNote: The characters' fall down the pyramid is depicted as increasingly chaotic acceleration; Chucky is described as falling 'like a helpless turtle on its back.'

comics.title: The Myriad Creatures of the Night

comics.commentHtml: Sorry I've been posting comics so infrequently lately but I've been very busy lately.........GETTING LAID!

transcript.scene: A nighttime scene with a dark blue starry sky, crescent moon, and silhouetted forest treeline. Waveform diagrams are displayed in the middle section, with a small illuminated bar at the bottom left and a final waveform at the very bottom.
transcript.text:
Have you ever listened to the sounds of nature at night?

Those are the sounds of the myriad creatures participating in the great chain of life. Each creature must heed its inner primal urge to scream desperately for the attention of a potential mate – an urge which was molded by millions of years of evolutionary pressure.

If you listen carefully, you might be able to make out the specific songs of the different creatures.

The beautiful melody of the birds.

The hypnotic chirping of the crickets.

The massed chorus of the cicadas.

If you listen at the right time of night, these sounds can reach a deafening crescendo.

So the next time you're out at night, listen carefully to the beautiful symphony emanating from the darkness.

Those are the sounds of the myriad creatures of the night seeking love.
transcript.visualNote: Waveform diagrams labeled with different nocturnal creatures contrast romantic language about nature's "beautiful symphony" with increasingly dense audio visualizations. The cicadas' waveform is the most chaotic, emphasizing overwhelming noise rather than beauty. The final waveform from inside the bar suggests humans also participate in this same primal nighttime ritual.

comics.title: subfield

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure interview between two characters at a desk. The comic alternates between dialogue panels and explanatory text blocks with scientific diagrams, equations, molecular structures, and biological illustrations.
transcript.text:
SO YOUR RÉSUMÉ SAYS THAT YOU MAJORED IN MATHEMATICS.
YEAH, THAT'S RIGHT.

WELL, ACTUALLY I MAJORED IN PHYSICS. BUT PHYSICS IS JUST A SUBFIELD OF MATHEMATICS.
WHAT?

When you look at our universe at its most fundamental level, everything is defined purely and completely through equations and numbers.

R_μν - 1/2 R g_μν = 8πG T_μν
[−ℏ²/2m ∇² + V]ψ = iℏ ∂ψ/∂t
|↑↓| = 2

U, E, I, E, G, H
J, S, U, Y
E, H, T, Z
X, Y, Z, W

One can literally conceive of an infinite number of mathematical universes, but our universe just happens to be a particular solution to one.

BUT... THAT'S ... JUST LIKE MY OPINION.
OKAY, SO YOU WERE A PHYSICS MAJOR.
YEP.
WELL, NO. ACTUALLY I WAS A CHEMISTRY MAJOR.

But when you think about it, chemistry is all about the physics of atoms and the interactions of molecules. It's really just a subfield of physics and hence a subfield of math.

If you want to be really technical, I suppose I was a biology major. Since biology is really just the study of the chemical reactions and processes of living systems, I suppose you could say that it is a subfield of chemistry and hence a subfield of physics and hence a subfield of math.

IN PARTICULAR, I MAJORED IN NEUROSCIENCE.
LET'S JUST SKIP TO THE END, SHALL WE?
WHAT DOES IT SAY ON YOUR DIPLOMA?
COMPARATIVE LITERATURE.
transcript.visualNote: The final panel reveals the diploma lists Comparative Literature, a discipline outside the STEM reduction chain argued throughout the comic.

transcript.scene: A three-panel stick figure comic in simple black and white line art with blue coloring on the characters' heads. transcript.text:
When I was little, I always wanted to catch a cute bunny and make it my pet.
I WILL CATCH HIM, AND I WILL HUG HIM AND PET HIM AND SQUEEZE HIM...

But, alas, the bunnies always escaped.
However, that's not the real tragedy of this story.

BAAAAWAGLHAR
Always try to see things from the other person's perspective.
transcript.visualNote: The innocent childhood fantasy of catching and hugging a bunny is reframed as a terrifying predator chase from the bunny's point of view. The bunny's demonic appearance with horns and wild eyes, combined with the onomatopoeia 'BAAAWAGLHAR,' emphasizes the horror of being hunted by a much larger creature.

Panel 7: "MAYBE I SHOULD ALSO ACCIDENTALLY LEAVE OUT MY DVD OF 'THE NOTEBOOK'."

Panel 8: "THAT WAY SHE'LL KNOW THAT I'M A SENSITIVE GUY AND STUFF."

Panel 9: "BUT WHAT IF THAT MAKES ME LOOK TOO WIMPY?"

Panel 10: "MAYBE I SHOULD FORGET TO PUT AWAY MY DUMBBELLS."

Final Panel: "WHAT IS THAT?" / "THE INNER WORKINGS OF MY MIND."
transcript.visualNote: The final panel shows someone riding a bicycle through a shopping cart filled with scattered items, visually representing the character's chaotic and contradictory thoughts.

comics.title: the underworld
comics.comment: *applauds*
comics.commentHtml: *applauds*

transcript.scene: A stick-figure character receives a job orientation in an underground facility filled with machinery and libraries. The minimalist black and white comic uses detailed mechanical illustrations and architectural elements to depict the setting.
transcript.text:
WELCOME TO YOUR JOB ORIENTATION IN THE UNDERWORLD.

I THINK YOU'LL FIND THAT WE HAVE EVERYTHING YOU NEED TO MAKE YOU AS PRODUCTIVE AS POSSIBLE.

DIRECTLY UNDERNEATH US IS THE GREAT MACHINERY THAT DRIVES THIS AMAZING PLACE.

IT IS WHAT WILL ALLOW YOU TO COMPLETE YOUR TASKS SEEMINGLY SEAMLESSLY.

YOU WILL ALSO HAVE ACCESS TO ALL OF THESE GREAT LIBRARIES.

WITH THE RIGHT TOOLS, YOU CAN UTILIZE ALL OF THESE GREAT RESOURCES TO CREATE ANY WORLD YOU WANT.

...ANY MAGNIFICENT FANTASTICAL WORLD THAT YOUR BRAIN CAN CONCEIVE OF.

YOU WILL BE THE CREATORS OF VAST UNIVERSES THAT CAN THEN BE SHARED WITH THE OUTSIDE WORLD.

YEAH!

BUT...UH, UNFORTUNATELY, THESE ARE OUR ONLY WIN-DOWS TO THE OUTSIDE WORLD.

Let's show some love for mobile game developers.
transcript.visualNote: The "underworld" is revealed as a game development studio metaphor. The final panel uses "windows" as a pun—simultaneously referring to software platforms and literal windows as the only connection to the outside world.

comics.title: Astronaut Barbie
comics.comment: Statistically, I have a better chance of becoming a supermodel than I do of becoming an astronaut.
comics.commentHtml: Statistically, I have a better chance of becoming a supermodel than I do of becoming an astronaut.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure comic strip depicting Barbie's journey from a struggling student to the first person on Mars, which she uses as a base to conquer Earth. Simple black lines on white backgrounds with red representing Mars.
transcript.text:
ONE SMALL STEP FOR WOMAN.
ONE GIANT LEAP FOR HUMANKIND.

And just like that, Barbie became the first person to set foot on Mars.

And Barbie began thinking about the long unlikely journey that had brought her from the dusty trailer park in the midwest to this dusty red planet millions of miles away.

...the many years she spent as a struggling student, working full-time to put herself through college.

But she did it. She became the first one in her hard-working, blue collar family to graduate from college.

In fact, she would go on to earn advanced degrees in astrophysics, molecular biology, and mechanical engineering.

The day she got selected by NASA for the Mars program was the proudest moment of her life.

Now she was walking on the surface of Mars and was finally in a position to execute her ultimate plan.

BARBIE, THIS IS MISSION CONTROL. WE HAVEN'T HEARD FROM YOU IN THREE DAYS. PLEASE RESPOND.

ITS AS I FEARED, BARBIE HAS CUT OFF ALL CONTACT. ASTRONAUT BARBIE HAS GONE ROGUE!!!

Yes, Mars was now Barbie's own personal evil lair. Free from the scrutiny of Earth, she quickly put her plan into motion.

First, she created an army of clones from some of her ex-boyfriend's DNA that she had smuggled onto the space craft.

Next, she built a powerful Tesla death ray machine.

Billions of lives were lost when she unleashed the death ray on Earth. But, for the survivors on Earth, their nightmare was just beginning.

With Earth's military forces greatly weakened by Barbie's preemptive strike, she began the invasion with her Ken army.

And the trembling throng cowered in fear at the sight of their new ruthless queen.

KNEEL! KNEEL BEFORE BARBIE!
transcript.visualNote: The narrative subverts an inspirational space exploration story by revealing Mars serves as Barbie's evil lair for global conquest. Tiny figures kneel before Barbie in the final panel, emphasizing her tyrannical power.

comics.title: The Too Much Information Age
comics.comment: I recently had smart gas, electric, and water meters installed. My loss of privacy is now complete.
comics.commentHtml: I recently had smart gas, electric, and water meters installed.  My loss of privacy is now complete.

transcript.scene: A six-panel webcomic in simple black-and-white line art showing four identical stick figures sitting at desks, each labeled as employees from water, cable internet, phone, and gas/electric utility companies, observing a customer's daily activities through usage data.
transcript.text:
Panel 1: SUDDEN INCREASE IN ELECTRICITY. LOOKS LIKE OUR BOY IS UP.

Panel 2: 3.5-GALLON SPIKE IN WATER USAGE. CONSISTENT WITH TOILET FLUSH. MORNING PISS CONFIRMED.

Panel 3: HE'S CHECKING TRAFFIC AND WEATHER ON CABLE BOX #2. THAT'S THE LIVING ROOM.

Panel 4: SUDDEN INCREASE IN GAS USAGE. MUST BE COOKING BREAKFAST ON THE STOVE.

Panel 5: MAN, THIS GUY HASN'T RECEIVED A SINGLE PHONE CALL IN FOUR DAYS. WHAT A LOSER. / HA HA HA HA | HA HA

Panel 6: HE'S BROWSING FLIPBOARD ON HIS SMARTPHONE.

Panel 7: AND ANOTHER 3.5-GALLON SPIKE IN WATER. MORNING SHIT CONFIRMED.

Caption: If these guys ever got together, they would know way too much about my daily routine.
transcript.visualNote: The humor relies on utility company employees narrating intimate details of a person's daily routine based solely on usage data patterns, with the phone company employee's comment about no calls being mocked by the others who can see the customer's actual smartphone activity.

* SIGH *

I HAVEN'T HAD INTERNET ACCESS FOR THREE DAYS NOW.

...LIKE A PHANTOM LIMB.

AND MY PHANTOM INTERNET FRIENDS ARE TELLING ME HOW I SHOULD FEEL ABOUT THINGS, WHAT'S WRONG WITH ME, DOC?

YES, I'VE SEEN THIS CONDITION BEFORE.

IT WOULD APPEAR THAT YOUR BRAIN IS STARTING TO THINK FOR ITSELF.
HOW DO I MAKE IT STOP?

comics.title: Karpathy's Basilisk
comics.comment: And that's my story... and my warning. I hope the code wasn't too buggy. It was a rush job. Actually, many of you really have been asking where I've been. It's kind of a long story. I never really planned to stop updating this site but sometimes life just happens. Anyway, now that I'm able to get back into the grind, I will be posting again. I probably won't be updating on a regular schedule (not right away anyway) but I will post when I can, so check back once in a while. In the meantime, remember to feed the goose to help keep me alive (literally). Stay awesome. GO EAGLES!
comics.commentHtml:
And that's my story... and my warning.

I hope the code wasn't too buggy. It was a rush job.

Actually, many of you really have been asking where I've been. It's kind of a long story. I never really planned to stop updating this site but sometimes life just happens.

Anyway, now that I'm able to get back into the grind, I will be posting again. I probably won't be updating on a regular schedule (not right away anyway) but I will post when I can, so check back once in a while.

In the meantime, remember to feed the goose to help keep me alive (literally).

Stay awesome.

GO EAGLES!

comics.title: Karpathy's Basilisk Reloaded
comics.comment: Since y'all seemed to like Karpathy's Basilisk I thought you might appreciate pitting your skills against the real thing. So over the weekend I became an 'expert' in Deep Reinforcement Learning (r/iamverysmart) and trained the basilisk minion to destroy your ass. Originally I tried training an agent to learn to play the Prime Porkour game . However, while it was learning to identify prime numbers, it accidentally discovered a polynomial-time factoring algorithm and if I ever released it to the public it would render useless 98.7 percent of the most popular cryptosystems in widespread use today and civilization as we know it would collapse. That's too much responsibility for me. To train the minion, I used a Reinforcement Learning library written by (you guessed it) @karpathy so, in a sense, you really are battling against Karpathy's Basilisk. The library is actually written in JavaScript. JavaScript! That's just... obscene. At least it's certainly not the way God intended it, but it seems to work well enough (as in it trains reasonably quickly i.e. it literally trained while I ate dinner). The minion was trained with a method called Deep Q Learning which, some of you may recall, was popularized in 2013 (ancient history in machine learning time) when some crazy-awesome people used it to train an agent to play Atari games using the game's raw pixels as input. However, the basilisk minion isn't quite that sophisticated. It was trained against a dummy player that just mindlessly moved towards the package. It observed its own position and velocity, the position and velocity of the player, and the position of the package. It was rewarded for minimizing the distance between itself and the player and it received a bonus for capturing the player. It was penalized if the player was able to acquire the package. Since the minion was trained against a mindless drone, it's pretty easy to confuse it sometimes by simpy making some random moves. However, if you took the time to play around with some of the parameters and stuff, I'm pretty sure that you could create an unbeatable minion. But what fun is that? PS: Thank you for all the variants of pi and e. Y'all know who you are. :)
comics.commentHtml:
Since y'all seemed to like Karpathy's Basilisk I thought you might appreciate pitting your skills against the real thing. So over the weekend I became an 'expert' in Deep Reinforcement Learning (r/iamverysmart) and trained the basilisk minion to destroy your ass. Originally I tried training an agent to learn to play the Prime Porkour game. However, while it was learning to identify prime numbers, it accidentally discovered a polynomial-time factoring algorithm and if I ever released it to the public it would render useless 98.7 percent of the most popular cryptosystems in widespread use today and civilization as we know it would collapse. That's too much responsibility for me.

To train the minion, I used a Reinforcement Learning library written by (you guessed it) @karpathy so, in a sense, you really are battling against Karpathy's Basilisk. The library is actually written in JavaScript. JavaScript! That's just... obscene. At least it's certainly not the way God intended it, but it seems to work well enough (as in it trains reasonably quickly i.e. it literally trained while I ate dinner).

The minion was trained with a method called Deep Q Learning which, some of you may recall, was popularized in 2013 (ancient history in machine learning time) when some crazy-awesome people used it to train an agent to play Atari games using the game's raw pixels as input. However, the basilisk minion isn't quite that sophisticated. It was trained against a dummy player that just mindlessly moved towards the package. It observed its own position and velocity, the position and velocity of the player, and the position of the package. It was rewarded for minimizing the distance between itself and the player and it received a bonus for capturing the player. It was penalized if the player was able to acquire the package.

Since the minion was trained against a mindless drone, it's pretty easy to confuse it sometimes by simpy making some random moves. However, if you took the time to play around with some of the parameters and stuff, I'm pretty sure that you could create an unbeatable minion. But what fun is that?

PS: Thank you for all the variants of pi and e. Y'all know who you are. :)

HOW THE HELL DID YOU BECOME THE WORLD'S FIRST TRILLIONAIRE?

IT WAS NO BIGGIE. I JUST WROTE A SIMPLE FUNCTION APPROXIMATION PROGRAM.

ONCE I GOT THE RIGHT PARAMETERS, ARTIFICIAL GENERAL INTELLIGENCE WAS BORN.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic with blue-and-white color palette. Two characters stand by a waterfront with an urban cityscape, moving through scenes of conversation to a final image of silhouettes dancing under a starry night sky.
transcript.text:
Look at all those people out there.

Each one of those dimly lit windows encloses its own secret. And behind the dimly opaque eyes of each one of the people living behind each one of those dimly lit windows—

...buried deep in the cavernous consciousness of each human being, there is an entire universe of mental activity. A universe of thoughts and memories.

Every memory, every feeling, but a tiny patch in a vast intricate tapestry of an individual's life experiences. And each tapestry is unique and more inscrutable than the next. In this city alone, there are millions of these tapestries overlapping, yet never really touching. I wonder, how many of those tapestries am I featured in? Even if only as a small insignificant speck on a tiny thread on a small patch long forgotten? Thousands? Millions? The prospect fills me with a palpable awe.

And I am even more awed by the thought that even this overwhelming feeling from the acute realization that every human being encloses a fathomless universe of secrets...even *that* feeling is unique, for no two people experience it in quite the same way, so I can never really fully share this feeling with anyone else.

Ultimately, we are all alone.

MIND BLOWN.

WHAT DID YOU SAY?
OMG, WERE YOU SONDERING JUST NOW, TOO?
ARE YOU KIDDING? I WAS SONDERING SO HARD!
transcript.visualNote: The characters' simultaneous realization of their shared profound thought undermines the preceding monologue about fundamental human isolation. The final panels show them embracing and dancing together, visually contradicting the spoken conclusion.

transcript.visualNote: Panel 9's caption echoes Panel 1, creating a recursive loop suggesting the character may themselves be simulated.

comics.title: The G O O D B O Y E Dilemma
comics.comment: When I was growing up, I always wanted to be considered fearsome like a lion but, apparently, I'm just cute like a puppy. I've learned to live with it.
comics.commentHtml: When I was growing up, I always wanted to be considered fearsome like a lion but, apparently, I'm just cute like a puppy. I've learned to live with it.

transcript.scene: A four-panel black silhouette comic of a human and dog in conversation. The dog becomes increasingly animated while articulating grievances about selective breeding.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
Human: "WHY DID YOU DO THIS TO ME?"
Dog: "ARF ARF ARF ARF"

Panel 2:
Dog: "YOU HUMANS HAVE BEEN SELFISHLY SUBJECTING US TO SELECTIVE BREEDING FOR THOUSANDS OF YEARS TO ACCENTUATE OUR CUTENESS AND TO PERPETUATE CERTAIN BEHAVIORAL TENDENCIES LIKE PLAYFULNESS, NON-AGGRESSION, AND UNCONDITIONAL LOVE!!! ARF ARF ARF"

Panel 3:
Dog: "YOU COULD HAVE BRED US TO BE FEROCIOUS BEASTS THAT STILL FEAR IN THE REST OF THE ANIMAL KINGDOM. I WANT TO BE A FEROCIOUS BEAST, DAMMIT!"
Human: "ARF ARF ARF"

Panel 4:
Dog: "I AM VERY UPSET WITH YOU RIGHT NOW FOR THE MORALLY QUESTIONABLE PRACTICES OF YOUR SPECIES BUT I JUST WANT TO PLAY WITH YOU ALL DAY AND I LOVE YOU SO SO MUCH!!!"
Human: "ARF ARF ARF"
transcript.visualNote: The dog articulates a philosophical complaint about selective breeding while being unable to suppress the very affectionate traits it is criticizing.

comics.title: witch hunt
comics.comment: The Phantom Tollbooth is NOT a children's book. Change my mind.
comics.commentHtml: The Phantom Tollbooth is NOT a children's book. Change my mind.

transcript.scene: Two torch-wielding stick figures confront a green-faced character wearing a witch hat across a 3x3 grid of panels. The art style is minimalist stick figures with simple black lines.
transcript.text:
BURN THE WITCH!
BURN THE WITCH!

WAIT, I'M NOT A WITCH.
I'M A WHICH.
WUT?

DIDN'T YOU GUYS READ
THE PHANTOM TOLLBOOTH
WHEN YOU
WERE KIDS?

I'M A WHICH. I'M IN CHARGE OF WHICH WORDS SHOULD BE USED FOR CERTAIN OCCASIONS, WHICH ONES TO SAY AND WHICH ONES NOT TO SAY, WHICH ONES TO WRITE AND WHICH ONES NOT TO WRITE.

I DO MY BEST TO MAKE SURE THAT ONLY THE MOST PROPER AND FIT-TING WORDS ARE USED.

WITHOUT ME, PEOPLE WOULDN'T BE ABLE TO HAVE INTELLIGENT DISCUSSIONS.

THE PROPER USE OF WORDS IS WHAT ALLOWS GOOD IDEAS TO BE PROPAGATED THROUGHOUT SOCIETY.

IT'S WHAT ALLOWS DIFFERENCES TO BE RESOLVED BETWEEN INDIVIDUALS AND BETWEEN DIFFERENT GROUPS, BETWEEN NATIONS.

DO YOU REALLY WANT TO RID THE WORLD OF THAT?

BURN THE WHICH!
I DON'T KNOW WHAT I WAS EXPECTING.
transcript.visualNote: The homophone 'witch/which' creates the central joke—the character is a guardian of proper word usage, not a supernatural being, making the mob's conclusion ironic.

comics.title: patently...
comics.comment: All your convnet are belong to me.
comics.commentHtml: All your convnet are belong to me.

transcript.scene: An office setting with two stick figure characters, one with long hair and one wearing glasses, discussing a patent application. The comic is rendered in black and white with a minimalist style and progresses from casual conversation to escalating frustration.
transcript.text:
PATENT APPLICATION
Applying a nonlinear transformation to the output of a linear transformation.

Abstract:
Methods, systems, and apparatus, including computer programs encoded on computer media storage, for applying a nonlinear transformation to the output of any of a plurality of linear transformations wherein the nonlinear transformation is a piecewise linear function that returns the positive values from a plurality of real numbers from the domain of said piecewise linear function wherein a plurality of...

WAIT, ARE YOU TRYING TO PATENT THE ReLU ACTIVATION FUNCTION?

YEAH, I CAN'T BELIEVE NOBODY THOUGHT TO DO THIS BEFORE.

JUST THINK ABOUT HOW MANY ELEMENT-WISE ReLU COMPUTATIONS WILL BE PERFORMED IN EVERY FORWARD PASS OF EVERY DEEP NET THAT WILL BE DEPLOYED IN THE NEXT 10 YEARS AND...

ARE YOU CRAZY?!!

?

ReLU IS JUST A FANCY NAME FOR USING A SIMPLE ACTIVATION FUNCTION! YOU'RE ESSENTIALLY TRYING TO PATENT A BASIC MATHEMATICAL CONCEPT!

WOULD YOU TRY TO PATENT THE SINE FUNCTION?! OR LONG DIVISION?! OR THE EUCLIDEAN ALGORITHM?!!

BUT...

THIS ISN'T WHAT THE PATENT SYSTEM WAS DESIGNED FOR! PATENTS ARE SUPPOSED TO ENCOURAGE INNOVATION, NOT STIFLE IT!

YOU CAN'T JUST PATENT MATH!!!

NOT WITH THAT ATTITUDE.
transcript.visualNote: The final panel's humor comes from the character responding to the statement "You can't just patent math" with "Not with that attitude," treating a mathematical impossibility as if it were merely a matter of determination.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure diner scene in black and white where a man in a blue cap sits at a restaurant table with other patrons. The comic progresses through eight panels documenting an escalating social embarrassment.

YES, THAT WILL DO NICELY. THANK YOU.

HAHA

MAN TRIES TO IMPRESS DATE WITH WINE EMBARRASSES SELF

comics.title: miracle fly
comics.comment: Whenever a fly gets in my house, I put my life on hold and I will literally spend hours obsessively hunting that little guy because... ew.
comics.commentHtml: Whenever a fly gets in my house, I put my life on hold and I will literally spend hours obsessively hunting that little guy because... ew.

transcript.scene: A simple black-and-white stick figure comic where two characters debate whether to kill a housefly that has entered the house, beginning with moral arguments before shifting to detailed scientific facts about fly biology.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
Character 1: "HEY, A FLY GOT IN YOUR HOUSE."
Character 2: "NO, DON'T KILL IT."

Panel 2:
Character 2: "YOU CAN'T JUST DESTROY THIS MIRACLE OF NATURE THAT TOOK MILLIONS OF YEARS TO EVOLVE."

Panel 3:
Character 1: "THE HOUSEFLY IS AN ELEGANTLY DESIGNED MARVEL, SUPERB IN EFFICIENCY, YET BREATHTAKING IN ITS SOPHISTICATION."
Character 2: "OKAY."

Panel 4:
Character 1: "DID YOU KNOW THAT THEIR COMPOUND EYES ARE PERFECTLY CONSTRUCTED TO DETECT EVEN THE SLIGHTEST MOVEMENT IN ITS VICINITY AND BOAST A CRITICAL FLICKER FUSION RATE OVER FOUR TIMES OURS? THAT'S WHY THEY'RE SO HARD TO SWAT. THEY BASICALLY SEE THE WORLD IN SLO-MO COMPARED TO US."

Panel 5:
Character 1: "AND DID YOU KNOW THAT FLIES HAVE CHEMORECEPTORS ON THE ENDS OF THEIR LEGS SO THEY CAN TASTE THEIR FOOD JUST BY WALKING ON IT?"
Character 2: "I DID NOT KNOW THAT."

Panel 6:
Character 1: "AND FLIES HAVE A FLEXIBLE, RETRACTABLE PROBOSCIS WHICH CAN HOOVER UP THEIR LIQUID FOOD VIA CAPILLARY ACTION."
Character 1: "TRULY INGENIOUS DESIGN."

Panel 7:
Character 1: "ON THE OTHER HAND, THAT FLY PROBABLY SPENT ALL DAY WALKING ON SHIT AND NOW IT'S INSIDE YOUR HOUSE."

Panel 8:
(No dialogue)

Panel 9:
(No dialogue)
transcript.visualNote: The final panel shows the house engulfed in flames with both characters standing outside, an absurdly disproportionate response to the revelation that the fly was contaminated.

comics.title: pop phys
comics.comment: I felt a great disturbance in the Force, as if millions of physicists and mathematicians suddenly groaned in horror.
comics.commentHtml: I felt a great disturbance in the Force, as if millions of physicists and mathematicians suddenly groaned in horror.

transcript.scene: A stick-figure presenter with glasses stands at a podium addressing an audience of silhouetted figures in a 10-panel black and white comic. The presenter discusses the challenges of writing popular physics books while dealing with increasingly vocal critics.
transcript.text:
SO I'M TRYING TO WRITE A POPULAR PHYSICS BOOK FOR LAYPEOPLE, AND IT'S NOT AS EASY AS IT SOUNDS.

THE TRICK IS TO RESIST THE TEMPTATION TO TRY TO EXPLAIN EVERYTHING FULLY. MOST READERS ARE CONTENT WITH JUST AN INTUITIVE UNDERSTANDING OF THE CONCEPTS.

SEE, HERE I'M TRYING TO EXPLAIN STANDARD CALCULUS BY SAYING THAT WE CAN THINK OF 'dy' AS A TINY CHANGE IN THE VALUE OF y AND 'dx' AS THE CORRESPONDING TINY CHANGE IN x.

GROAN
CRINGE
GROAN

WHO THE HELL ARE THOSE GUYS?
THEY'RE SCIENTISTS AND MATHEMATICIANS.

THEY BITCH AND MOAN EVERY TIME I FORGO RIGOR AND WRITE SOMETHING THAT ISN'T EXACTLY TECHNICALLY CORRECT.

NOW, HERE IN CHAPTER 3, I DO A STEP-BY-STEP HEURISTIC DERIVATION OF THE FREE PARTICLE SCHRÖDINGER EQUATION.
WHOA, WTF?

IS HE SERIOUS?
NOW WHAT ARE THEY COMPLAINING ABOUT?

SOMETIMES THEY GO THE OTHER WAY. SOME STEPS OF THE DERIVATION ARE SO OBVIOUS TO THEM THAT THEY THINK IT'S RIDICULOUS TO INCLUDE THEM.

IT'S HARD TO PLEASE ALL OF THEM.

NOW, HERE, I EXPLAIN THAT THE QUANTUM MECHANICAL SPIN OF A PARTICLE IS LIKE A BASKETBALL SPINNING ON ITS AXIS.
OOOH... TSK TSK TSK

OKAY, THESE GUYS ARE BEGINNING TO CREEP ME OUT.
YOU CANNOT BE SERIOUS.
JUST IGNORE THEM. THEY REALLY ONLY EXIST IN YOUR MIND.
transcript.visualNote: The scientists appear visually in the audience despite the final panel's claim that they only exist in the presenter's mind.

transcript.scene: A minimalist black and white comic featuring a long-haired stick figure and a round character wearing a blue cap across eight panels. The long-haired figure encounters the blue-capped character and attempts to apologize for a past incident.

Blue-capped figure: "OH... HI."

transcript.scene: A black and white minimalist courtroom with a judge's bench and counsel tables depicted in a grid of panels using stick-figure art. The scene escalates from mathematical argumentation to emotional rhetoric, culminating in an absurd verdict.
transcript.text:
Panel 1:
[Mathematical equations on chalkboard behind judge]
P(Ω)={1}}135.2
k₂(2g,s)·s=s·s, β=σp_g·3>a⊗β·2...=σnβ=Λ
k₂(3),s|1,34 +3D1.3†, Fᵥₚₚ

Panel 2:
FROM THIS PROPOSITION IT WILL FOLLOW, WHEN ARITHMETICAL ADDITION HAS BEEN DEFINED, THAT 1+1=2.

Panel 3:
THANK YOU, COUNSELOR. LET'S HEAR THE OPPOSING ARGUMENT.

Panel 4:
I AM OUTRAGED AND APPALLED AT THE OPPOSING SIDE'S PERVERSE USE OF LOGIC AND MATH!

Panel 5:
THIS IS AN INSULT TO OUR GREAT COUNTRY, OUR BRAVE TROOPS, AND OUR FAMILY VALUES!!!

Panel 6:
WE SAY THAT 1+1=3!!!

Panel 7:
AND IF YOU DON'T LIKE IT, YOU SUNBITCHES CAN GET THE HELL OUTTA THE COUNTRY!!!

Panel 8:
THANK YOU, COUNSELORS. THE COURT WILL NOW DELIBERATE ON THE MERITS OF THE CASE.

Panel 9:
IT IS THE OPINION OF THIS COURT THAT EXTREME MORAL OUTRAGE IS MORE EFFECTIVE THAN DEDUCTIVE REASONING.

Panel 10:
THE COURT HAS CONCLUDED, 5 TO 4, THAT 1 PLUS 1 CAN INDEED EQUAL 3 IF YOU'RE ANGRY ENOUGH.

Panel 11:
AND, BY EXTENSION, THE USUAL ORDER RELATION ON THE INTEGERS IS EFFECTIVELY RENDERED MEANINGLESS. IT IS SO ORDERED.

WHOO HOO! WHOO HOO!
THIS IS AN OUTRAGE.
transcript.visualNote: The verdict replaces mathematical proof with emotional intensity as the basis for legal judgment, with celebration and protest in the final panel.

comics.title: The Most Powerful Person in the World - part 2

transcript.scene: A four-panel stick figure comic in black-and-white, depicting escalating hazards from a UFO abduction to earthly dangers, concluding with a silhouetted horseman on a hillside.
transcript.visualNote: The comic presents an escalating sequence: peaceful alien abduction, ejection from the saucer, being dropped by hands, attack by a mounted warrior, and finally a horseman struck by lightning, suggesting ordinary earthly hazards pose greater danger than extraterrestrial encounter.

comics.title: as surely as the sun rises...
comics.comment: As surely as the sun rises, philosophers will argue about the induction problem.
comics.commentHtml: As surely as the sun rises, philosophers will argue about the induction problem.

transcript.scene: A two-column stick-figure webcomic. Left side shows an angel and smaller character with an angel observing a universe expiring and discarding it into fire. Right side depicts two characters conversing in a pastoral farm setting with sunrise.
transcript.text:
Left Column:
BEST BY 13,814,866,948
Lord, this universe is about to go bad.
hmmm. So it is.
Throw it in the incinerator.
Yes, Lord.

Right Column:
I LOVE WAITING FOR THE SUN TO RISE EVERY MORNING.
DID YOU EVER HEAR THE STORY OF THE INDUCTIVIST TURKEY?
WHAT?
IT'S A STORY THAT IS OFTEN CITED TO ILLUSTRATE THE PROBLEM OF INDUCTION.
Y'SEE, ON ITS FIRST DAY ON THE FARM, THE TURKEY GOT FED AT EXACTLY 1 PM.
SO NATURALLY THE TURKEY CONCLUDED THAT IT WOULD GET FED AT 1 AM EVERY DAY.
AND EVERY DAY FOR THE NEXT SEVERAL MONTHS THE TURKEY GOT FED AT 1 AM.
BUT THANKSGIVING ARRIVED AND IT GOT ITS THROAT CUT.
A REASONABLE TURKEY.
WHAT A HORRIBLE STORY.
transcript.visualNote: The expiration date shown is an absurdly large number, implying the universe itself has a shelf life. The story of the turkey's inductive reasoning—inferring safety from repeated daily feeding—parallels the characters' obliviousness to the universe's impending destruction occurring simultaneously in the left panels.

transcript.scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic with black backgrounds. Two characters sit across from each other at a table: one with black hair on the left, one depicted as a simple circle on the right.

"FROM BEYOND WHERE? THIS DIMENSION. HE NOW EXISTS IN HIGHER DIMENSIONS."

Panel 3:
"HIGHER DIMENSIONS?"
"YES."

transcript.visualNote: The character confidently uses scientific terminology (higher dimensions, vibrational frequency, life-force energy, gauge anomaly) in nonsensical ways, then admits the words mean something entirely different to them before abruptly dismissing the other character.
